title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The method specifically addresses the challenges posed by the inherent sparsity of LiDAR data in dynamic environments, a problem highlighted by the statistics presented in Figure 1, which show that a significant percentage of objects (e.g., 51% of KITTI cars have less than 100 points) have very few points. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area by generating dense geometric features; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network, an anchor-free approach, to better localize 3D objects by regressing the 3D center from a dense 2D feature map. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts on widely used benchmarks like KITTI and nuScenes, and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

### Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds, which is crucial for applications like autonomous driving and mobile robotics. The inherent sparsity of LiDAR data, as quantitatively illustrated in Figure 1, makes distinguishing targets from background a significant challenge that this work directly tackles. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided, particularly in Section 3 where the architecture of the Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network (Sec. 3.1) and the Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization Network (Sec. 3.2) are meticulously described, aided by clear diagrams in Figures 2 and 3. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound, presenting a coherent and well-motivated architecture from feature extraction to target localization. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims. For instance, the overall superior performance on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets (Table 1) strongly supports the effectiveness of the combined approach. The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed, especially in Section 2, where the authors effectively position V2B against pioneering 3D SOT methods like SC3D and P2B, highlighting improvements such as end-to-end training and avoiding low-quality proposals. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell, providing a comprehensive overview of 2D and 3D tracking, as well as deep learning on point clouds. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin. For example, on the challenging KITTI dataset, V2B improves the 'Car' category Success metric from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5%, representing a substantial gain, and demonstrates good generalization ability on the nuScenes dataset (Table 1).

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 (all frames) and Table 2 (sparse scenarios), when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop (e.g., for 'Car', Success drops from 70.5% to 64.7%) while some baseline methods demonstrate less severe drops or even slight gains for certain categories when switching to sparse scenes (e.g., BAT's 'Car' Success slightly increases from 60.5% to 60.7%, and for 'Pedestrian', P2B's Success increases from 28.7% to 33.1%). This observed trend, where V2B's performance degrades more noticeably in sparse scenes compared to its performance on all frames, challenges the core claim that its shape-aware feature learning or BEV localization makes it *more suitable* for sparse point clouds. While the authors explain this by suggesting that SC3D and P2B's worse performance on sparse frames impacts their overall performance on dense frames (lines 359-361), the direct comparison in Table 2 still shows a significant drop for V2B from its overall performance, making it not entirely clear whether the claimed shape-aware feature truly makes the proposed method more robust to sparsity than its competitors, especially when directly evaluating on sparse subsets.

Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module (Section 3.1.1) and in the shape-aware feature learning module (Section 3.1.2). Table 3, while demonstrating the individual impact of 'local temple' and 'global temple' when compared to the 'w/o template feature' baseline (which removes both), does not explicitly show the performance if *only* one of these branches (e.g., only local, or only global) were used, versus the combined approach. The same applies to the 'local geometric' and 'global shape' branches within the shape-aware feature learning module, where 'w/o shape information' removes the entire module rather than isolating the contribution of each branch when both are present. This makes it difficult to ascertain the synergistic effect and necessity of combining both types of features.

Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories (Car, Pedestrian, Van, Cyclist) should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. While the 'Car' category often provides a good general indicator due to its abundance in datasets, validating the findings from Table 3 (module effectiveness, e.g., template feature embedding, shape-aware feature learning) and Table 4 (detection schemes, voxel sizes, template generation) across more diverse object types would significantly strengthen the generalizability and robustness of the architectural choices.

### Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (specifically, the claim of being better at handling sparse points, and the necessity of using both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module due to incomplete ablation studies). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 from Table 5's discussion: "Compared with these methods, our V2B can yield stable results on four template generation schemes, which further demonstrates that our method can consistently generate accurate tracking results in all types of frames." While V2B indeed achieves high accuracy, the statement "stable does not mean accurate" is a valid logical critique; a method could be consistently stable but consistently wrong. The phrasing should be refined to clearly distinguish between stability (low variance) and accuracy (low bias). Finally, the paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal. The reliance on max-pooling along the z-axis to create a dense BEV feature map (Section 3.2.1) inherently compresses or discards fine-grained vertical information. In complex indoor environments with multi-level structures, stacked objects, or highly varied object heights and orientations, this loss of z-axis context could severely hinder accurate object localization and tracking, as the 2D BEV projection might become ambiguous.

### Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker, named Voxel-to-BEV (V2B), specifically designed for single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The method localizes a target object given its initial template and a small search space within a LiDAR scan. The proposed model largely consists of two primary modules: a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input, learning discriminative features that capture 3D shape information of the object. This network focuses on enhancing the correlation to the template point clouds and generating dense point clouds, which is particularly beneficial for sparse scenes. The resulting enhanced search space feature is then fed into the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network, which flattens the 3D voxelized point clouds into a Bird's Eye View (BEV) 2D feature map where the target localization, including 2D center, z-axis center, and rotation, happens in an anchor-free manner. The proposed method is evaluated on the challenging KITTI and nuScenes datasets, demonstrating that it significantly outperforms preceding works by a noticeable margin, particularly in sparse point cloud environments.

**Main Review:**
Overall, while this paper presents a compelling approach that clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods in terms of tracking performance and offers practical benefits regarding computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, the true novelty of certain architectural components, and the depth of the ablation study for its complex modules.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods by a considerable margin. For instance, on the KITTI dataset, V2B improves the Success metric for the 'Car' category from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5%, representing a substantial gain as shown in Table 1. The approach also demonstrates good generalization ability, achieving competitive results on the nuScenes dataset with models trained solely on KITTI, as detailed in Table 1. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, providing a clear advancement over existing techniques like SC3D, P2B, and BAT, especially in sparse scenarios (Table 2). The partial ablation study presented in Tables 3, 4, and 5 effectively supports the usefulness of the proposed components, such as template feature embedding, shape-aware feature learning, and the voxel-to-BEV localization scheme. Furthermore, the design choice to perform the shape generation task only during the training phase means it does not increase the inference time of object tracking during testing, which is a positive aspect for real-world applicability. I find it particularly interesting that a point cloud generation task, used exclusively during the training phase through the `Lshape` loss (Equation 6), helps to improve the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for subsequent localization, allowing the model to learn dense geometric features of the potential target even from sparse inputs.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper that temper my enthusiasm:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences throughout the manuscript. For example, in the abstract, "which can significantly improve the tracking performance in sparse 3D point clouds" is a bit redundant. In the Introduction, "Object tracking is an essential task in computer vision and has been widely in various applications" is grammatically incomplete. Another instance is "we hence consider compressing the sparse 3D space into a dense 2D space, and perform center regression in the dense 2D space to improve tracking performance," which could be phrased more elegantly. Such issues are prevalent and detract from the overall readability and professionalism of the paper. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread by a native English speaker to address these pervasive language issues before publication.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Network (Section 3.1.1, illustrated in the left half of Figure 3) is a huge and complicated module with heavy engineering of features without sufficient detailed ablation study in itself. The network combines both global shape information (Equation 2) and local similarity (Equation 4) using MLPs, max pooling, and cosine distance, followed by concatenation. While I do see a motivation behind the detailed design of this network, aiming to strengthen the correlation between template and search area, the complexity of its internal mechanisms is not properly backed by a detailed ablation study compared to its intricate design. For example, the current ablation in Table 3 only shows the overall impact of "w/o template feature," and separate results for "local temple" and "global temple," which is a good start. However, it does not explore alternative formulations for the correlation function `fcorr(pi, qj)` (Equation 1) or the similarity function `fsim(pi, qj)` (Equation 3), nor does it provide insights into different strategies for combining these global and local features. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to fully appreciate heavy engineering without a more thorough theoretical or empirical investigation into the specific design choices and their individual contributions.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network (Section 3.2, right half of Figure 2) seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection, and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. The methodology involves converting point features into a volumetric representation, applying 3D convolutions, and then performing max-pooling along the z-axis to obtain a dense BEV feature map. This BEV map is then processed by 2D convolutions, followed by anchor-free heads for 2D center, offset & rotation, and z-axis regression. This architectural pattern, particularly the transformation from sparse 3D points to a dense BEV grid via voxelization and subsequent 2D processing, is a well-established technique in the field of 3D object detection. This paper must cite foundational papers such as PointPillar [56] and SECOND [57, 58], which are commonly used 3D object detection encoders that extensively utilize this voxel-to-BEV structure for efficient processing and localization. While the paper does cite AFDet [19] for the anchor-free detection heads, the core BEV feature generation pipeline itself is a standard approach that should be appropriately referenced to acknowledge its origins.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitations or societal impact of this work within the main body of the paper. While there is a limited study of failure cases mentioned in the supplementary material, a more comprehensive discussion in the main paper would be beneficial. As a tracking problem, I believe this approach, like many 3D single object trackers, has numerous limitations such as its dependency on the initial bounding box, and challenges in handling situations like re-identification (e.g., if the object leaves and re-enters the field of view, or if the initial template is poor), and robust tracklet initialization in real-world, unconstrained scenarios. Other limitations could include performance degradation under heavy occlusion, very fast ego-motion, or when the target object undergoes extreme shape deformations not captured by the template or learned shape priors. A couple of sentences discussing the societal impact of this work, particularly in the context of autonomous vehicles (its primary application), seems to be expected by the conference as well. This should include considerations for safety, ethical implications, potential biases in training data, and privacy concerns related to LiDAR data collection.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper introduces a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) object tracker specifically designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds, a highly challenging task in autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture, comprising a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network, demonstrates significant robustness to sparse point cloud inputs and can substantially improve tracking performance. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks and nuScenes dataset strongly suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective and possesses good generalization capabilities.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task. The inherent sparsity of LiDAR point clouds, especially for distant or small objects, makes reliable 3D object tracking difficult. The authors effectively address this by introducing a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network (Section 3.1) to capture dense 3D shape information, which is crucial for distinguishing potential targets from background clutter. Furthermore, the voxel-to-BEV target localization network (Section 3.2) provides an elegant anchor-free solution for localizing targets in dense 2D BEV space, circumventing the issues of low-quality proposals prevalent in sparse 3D point clouds. This holistic approach makes the algorithm particularly well-suited for real-world autonomous driving scenarios where point cloud sparsity is a common issue.

- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin. As demonstrated in Table 1, V2B significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods like SC3D, P2B, and BAT across multiple categories on the KITTI dataset. For instance, in the "Car" category, V2B achieves a Success rate of 70.5% compared to BAT's 60.5%, representing a substantial improvement. The performance gain is also notable in sparse scenarios (Table 2), where V2B maintains strong performance (e.g., 64.7% Success for "Car" with ≤150 points) compared to other methods, underscoring its effectiveness in challenging conditions. The quantitative results on the nuScenes dataset (Table 1) further support the claim of good generalization ability, showing V2B consistently achieving better mean results (25.8%/29.0% Success/Precision) than its counterparts when models trained on KITTI are directly applied.

- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. The architectural overview in Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the flow from template and search area processing to feature learning and target localization. Detailed diagrams in Fig. 3 provide a clear breakdown of the template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning modules. Moreover, the visualization results in Fig. 4 effectively demonstrate V2B's superior tracking accuracy compared to P2B in both sparse and dense scenes, providing strong qualitative evidence for the method's claims.

- The authors explicitly state that the code is available at https://github.com/fpthink/V2B in the abstract. This commitment to open-sourcing the implementation is highly commendable, especially since this is an application paper with strong empirical results. The availability of the code will be invaluable for future research projects, enabling reproducibility, benchmarking, and further development by the wider academic community.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. While V2B generally outperforms other methods, its performance on "Cyclist" (40.8% Success/49.7% Precision on KITTI, Table 1; 30.4% Success/37.2% Precision on sparse KITTI, Table 2) is often lower than for "Car" or "Pedestrian". The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data for this category (only 308 frames for Cyclist vs. 6424 for Car in KITTI). Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same? It would be beneficial for the authors to elaborate on the inherent challenges of tracking cyclists, beyond just data scarcity, such as their potentially complex shapes, varied postures, or smaller point cloud representations even when present. A discussion on how the proposed shape-aware feature learning might be affected by extremely limited or highly variable shape data would be insightful.

- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. While the static visualization results in Fig. 4 are useful for illustrating specific frames, dynamic video demonstrations would provide a much more intuitive understanding of the tracker's temporal consistency, handling of occlusions, and overall robustness across sequences. Such videos could be hosted on a project page or in the supplementary material to better convey the method's real-time performance and stability.

- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed. Clarifying the purpose of the color coding, or simplifying it if it doesn't convey specific information, would improve the clarity of this important architectural diagram.

- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset for most of its ablation studies, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). For example, the loss function hyperparameters `λ1, λ2, and λ3` (set to `10^-6, 1.0, and 2.0` respectively in Section 3.2.2) and the optimal voxel size (found to be `0.3 meters` in Section 4.3) are crucial for performance. What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets or significantly different sensor configurations? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios without extensive re-tuning? While the nuScenes results are a good step towards demonstrating generalization, a more in-depth discussion on the robustness of these hyperparameter choices across diverse environments would strengthen the paper.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? Given the critical nature of autonomous driving, leveraging all available sensor modalities often leads to more robust and safer systems. While the current method focuses on LiDAR's robustness to sparse points, integrating visual cues from RGB cameras could further enhance tracking accuracy, particularly in scenarios with poor LiDAR returns (e.g., highly reflective surfaces, dark objects, or very distant objects) or for finer semantic understanding. My guess is that the current algorithm's Siamese architecture and feature learning components can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, perhaps by incorporating image features into the Siamese network or fusing them at a later stage. It would be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about this potential multi-modal extension and its implications for enhanced safety and performance in real-world autonomous driving systems.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper tackles the challenging problem of single-object tracking from sparse point clouds, a critical task in dynamic environments for applications like autonomous vehicles. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To address the inherent sparsity of LiDAR data, which the authors highlight with statistics showing 51% of KITTI cars having less than 100 points (Fig. 1), the method introduces two key components. First, to improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embedded into the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. Second, in terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal-based methods that struggle with low-quality proposals from sparse point clouds, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) voxel locations. Experiments on the KITTI dataset with a custom split, as well as generalization tests on nuScenes, show significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods, particularly in sparse scenarios.

Main Review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1.  **Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.**
    The method proposed in the submission, termed V2B, is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [52] and SC3D [21]. In particular, the overall two-step framework, involving a Siamese network for feature extraction followed by target localization, closely follows the design principles of P2B. The first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal-based method (Hough voting via VoteNet [48]), and this paper adopts a novel dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. The authors demonstrate this effectively in Table 4, where the proposed Voxel-to-BEV localization significantly outperforms VoteNet, particularly for 'Car' and 'Pedestrian' categories in sparse scenarios, showcasing its ability to overcome the limitations of proposal generation in sparse point clouds. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements, specifically illustrating cases where P2B's proposal generation fails due to sparsity while V2B successfully tracks the object.

    Another claimed novelty of shape-aware feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D [21], with the difference being the specific input feature (Lines 187-190). The manuscript explains that "our method is a complex point cloud generation method that learns the target completion model from the samples of search areas with the gate mechanism to enhance the feature of the potential target and suppress the background in the search area," contrasting it with SC3D's template completion model. Additionally, V2B's model constructs a global-local branch for feature extraction, which is a notable architectural difference from SC3D's use of PointNet. A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, for example, comparing V2B's shape-aware learning module directly against SC3D's template completion approach on the same backbone, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty. This would provide clearer empirical evidence for the specific improvements introduced by V2B's shape-aware feature learning, beyond the overall performance gain shown in Table 3 when comparing "w/o shape information" to the default setting.

    Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem, perhaps through a dedicated section or sub-section in the related work or method.

2.  **Needs better presentation of the method section.**
    It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. For instance, in Section 3.1 "Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network," the introduction of "Template Feature Embedding" (3.1.1) and "Shape-Aware Feature Learning" (3.1.2) could benefit from a clearer high-level overview of *why* these specific modules are necessary and how they contribute to solving the problem of sparse point clouds, before diving into the mathematical formulations of `fcorr`, `fsim`, and the gate mechanism. Similarly, for the "Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization Network" (Sec 3.2), the transition from "Dense BEV Feature Map Generation" (3.2.1) to "Target Localization in BEV" (3.2.2) could be motivated more explicitly by referencing the challenges of 3D center regression in sparse space, as mentioned in the Introduction ("it is difficult to regress the target center in 3D space. We hence consider compressing the sparse 3D space into a dense 2D space"). While the abstract and introduction provide some high-level motivation, carrying this into the sub-sections of the methodology would significantly improve readability and understanding. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission, perhaps by starting each subsection with a clear problem statement or a high-level explanation of its purpose before detailing the implementation.

3.  **More results in the experiments.**
    There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, specifically isolating the contribution of V2B's improved shape completion over SC3D's approach. Additionally, while Figure 4 provides visualization results for the 'car' category, a qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in *all four categories* (car, pedestrian, van, cyclist) would be highly beneficial, especially for the 'cyclist' category. The proposed method performs notably worse in this category compared with SC3D on the KITTI dataset (Table 1: V2B 40.8/49.7 vs SC3D 41.5/70.4, and even more so in sparse scenarios in Table 2: V2B 30.4/37.2 vs SC3D 50.2/69.2). Visualizing failure cases or challenging scenarios for cyclists where V2B underperforms its predecessor would provide valuable insights into the model's limitations. Also, in Table 3 (which details ablation studies for different components), the speed comparison is mentioned in the original review, stating that the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU. Any reason why this huge gap? This discrepancy in reported speeds across different hardware and possibly different evaluation setups needs to be clarified for a fair comparison of computational efficiency.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission. For instance, a discussion of limitations could include scenarios where V2B still struggles (e.g., extremely sparse point clouds beyond the evaluated thresholds, heavy occlusion, or specific object categories like 'cyclist' as noted in the results). The paper could also discuss the sensitivity to hyper-parameters like voxel size (as explored in experiments) or the number of points. For societal impact, given the application in autonomous driving, discussions around the safety implications of tracking errors, potential biases in training data (e.g., performance differences across object types or environmental conditions), and the computational resources required for deployment in real-world systems would be relevant.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single object tracking, specifically designed to address the challenges posed by sparse LiDAR point clouds in dynamic environments. The core architecture integrates several components: a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network, which includes both global and local template feature embedding, and a shape completion module to generate dense geometric features; and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that directly regresses the 3D target center in an anchor-free manner from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map. The authors claim this combination significantly improves tracking performance, particularly in sparse scenes.

**Main Review:**
The results look promising. The extensive evaluation on both the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrates that the proposed V2B method achieves state-of-the-art performance. For instance, on the KITTI dataset, the method significantly outperforms competitors like BAT, improving the average Success metric across four categories (car, pedestrian, van, cyclist) from 51.2% (BAT) to 58.4% (V2B), and Precision from 72.8% to 75.2% (Table 1). A particularly strong point is the method's performance in sparse scenarios, as highlighted in Table 2, where V2B again shows superior results, especially for categories like 'car' and 'pedestrian,' demonstrating its effectiveness in challenging low-point-count situations. The visualization results in Figure 4 also qualitatively support these claims, showing V2B's ability to accurately track targets in both sparse and dense scenes compared to P2B. The claim of good generalization ability is also partially supported by the nuScenes results, where V2B generally leads, albeit with lower absolute performance compared to KITTI.

My major concern is that the novelty is limited. The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (e.g., SiamRPN [1]), shape-completion, and BEV localization (e.g., LaserNet [2]). The "Siamese shape-aware feature learning network" (Section 3.1) combines a Siamese backbone (PointNet++), template feature embedding (global and local correlation), and a shape-aware feature learning module. While the paper argues for improvements over prior shape completion methods like SC3D [21] by using a more complex point cloud generation method with a global-local branch, the underlying concept of enhancing shape information for tracking is not entirely new. Similarly, the "voxel-to-BEV target localization network" (Section 3.2) relies on voxelization and max-pooling to create a dense BEV feature map for anchor-free regression, a strategy that draws parallels with existing 3D object detection architectures that leverage BEV representations for efficient localization. The paper's contribution largely lies in the specific integration and adaptation of these established techniques for the challenging domain of sparse 3D single object tracking.

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired. The reviewer notes that the paper already provides this in Table 3, under the "Template feature embedding" section. These results clearly demonstrate the individual contributions of the local and global branches, showing that "only using the local branch or global branch cannot achieve the best performance." The reported drops in Success/Precision (e.g., from 70.5/81.3 for the default setting to 68.0/79.2 for local temple only and 68.8/80.0 for global temple only) confirm the importance of combining both types of feature embedding for optimal tracking, effectively addressing this point of concern.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
Yes, there are limitations and societal impacts. One limitation is that while the method shows strong performance in sparse point clouds, the overall performance on the more diverse nuScenes dataset is considerably lower than on KITTI (e.g., Car Success 70.5% on KITTI vs. 31.3% on nuScenes). This suggests that while generalization exists, there might be inherent challenges in adapting to different sensor characteristics, scene complexities, or annotation styles across datasets, which is common in 3D perception. Another potential limitation, though mitigated by the authors' note, is the choice of PointNet++ as the backbone, which might not be the most powerful point cloud feature extractor available today, even if it's shared for efficiency. The claim that "shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing" for inference speed is a good design choice, but the complexity of the shape generation network (Sec 3.1.2) could still imply a significant training overhead.

From a societal impact perspective, this work directly contributes to improving the reliability of autonomous vehicles and robotics. Enhanced 3D object tracking, especially in sparse point clouds, is crucial for safe navigation, collision avoidance, and understanding dynamic environments. By making tracking more robust in challenging conditions (e.g., far-away objects, occlusions, adverse weather affecting LiDAR returns), the method can contribute to safer autonomous systems. However, as with all AI-driven safety-critical applications, the potential for failure modes in unforeseen corner cases or biases introduced by training data necessitates thorough validation and ethical considerations before real-world deployment.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[1]Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.
[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method, termed V2B, designed specifically to address the challenges of sparse LiDAR points in dynamic environments. The core of the proposed method involves a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker, which integrates a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning by generating dense geometric features. Furthermore, it employs an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV localization approach to directly regress the target's 3D center. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines, with additional evaluation on nuScenes showcasing its generalization ability.

**Main Review:**

**Paper Strengths**

1.  The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments.
    The template feature embedding, as detailed in Section 3.1.1 and illustrated in Figure 3, cleverly combines both global shape information (via max pooling across template points) and local geometric similarity (via cosine distance and nearest point assignment). This dual approach allows the model to capture a richer representation of the target, strengthening the correlation between the template and the search area. While the original review refers to Table 4, the ablation study explicitly demonstrating the impact of template feature embedding is found in Table 3 of the manuscript. As shown in Table 3, removing template feature embedding entirely ("w/o template feature") causes a substantial drop in performance from 70.5/81.3 to 63.9/73.9 (Success/Precision) on the car category. Furthermore, using only the local or global branches individually (68.0/79.2 and 68.8/80.0 respectively) also shows reduced performance compared to the combined approach, quantitatively supporting the effectiveness of integrating both global and local feature embeddings. This robust feature representation is critical for distinguishing the target from background clutter, especially in sparse point cloud settings.

2.  Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method.
    The visualization results presented in Figure 4 provide compelling qualitative evidence of V2B's superior performance, particularly in challenging sparse scenarios. The figure clearly contrasts V2B's accurate tracking (red boxes) against P2B's less reliable performance (blue boxes) across sequences with varying point densities. This visual comparison strongly supports the paper's central claim regarding its efficacy in sparse point clouds. Beyond visual appeal, this qualitative insight is backed by quantitative results in Table 2, which specifically evaluates performance in sparse scenes (e.g., cars with ≤150 points, pedestrians with ≤100 points). In these challenging conditions, V2B achieves a mean Success/Precision of 58.0/73.2, outperforming all baselines, including BAT (54.3/71.9) and P2B (47.2/63.5), further solidifying the claim that the method is particularly well-suited for sparse data.

**Paper Weaknesses**

1.  It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

    a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. It appears the original review intended to reference SC3D [21], which is discussed in the manuscript as a prior work utilizing template shape completion. The authors elaborate on their shape-aware feature learning in Section 3.1.2, stating that their method learns a target completion model from search area samples using a gate mechanism to enhance target features and suppress background. This is contrasted with SC3D [21], which uses a template completion model that may struggle with complex geometric structures due to limited templates and large variations in the search area (lines 100-107). Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, this table actually compares detection schemes. The relevant ablation is in Table 3, which shows that without the shape-aware feature learning module ("w/o shape information"), performance drops from 70.5/81.3 to 67.6/78.2 on the car category. While this demonstrates the module's benefit, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as done in SC3D [21]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea of search area completion is definitively better or worse than template completion. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and SC3D [21]'s approach) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for SC3D [21]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. While the paper suggests that search area completion with a gate mechanism handles variations better than template completion, a direct comparative experiment within the same framework would provide stronger evidence. Or maybe we should do both?

    b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. This process, described in Section 3.2.1 and depicted in Figure 2, is intended to create a "dense BEV feature map" from sparse 3D voxelized features. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? The paper claims that max-pooling along the z-axis suppresses low responses, making the highest response more distinct for 2D center localization (lines 64-67). However, in general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information, especially along an entire axis, so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. While it might simplify 2D localization, the loss of fine-grained vertical information through max-pooling seems counter-intuitive for precise z-axis regression. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding voxel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? A comparison of directly regressing the z-axis from the full 3D voxelized features versus from the z-axis max-pooled BEV features would be crucial to justify this design choice.

2.  The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017).
    While the authors state that PointNet++ "can be replaced with a powerful network such as KPConv [63]" (line 127), the choice to use PointNet++ as the primary backbone throughout the Siamese network (Section 3.1) and for feature extraction raises questions about the model's ultimate performance ceiling. Given the paper's emphasis on tackling sparse point clouds, where robust and fine-grained feature extraction is paramount, utilizing a more contemporary and powerful point cloud backbone (like KPConv [63] or other recent architectures mentioned in the related work, e.g., PointCNN [40]) could potentially yield even stronger results and demonstrate the full capabilities of the proposed V2B framework. A brief justification for selecting PointNet++ despite the availability of more advanced methods would be beneficial, perhaps in terms of computational efficiency or simplicity, if that was the primary motivation.

3.  Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol (scenes 19-20 for testing, line 169), but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. The number of frames for Van (1248) and especially Cyclist (308) on KITTI, as seen in Table 1, is indeed quite limited, and even more so in sparse scenarios (Table 2: 734 for Van, 59 for Cyclist), making the statistical significance for these categories questionable. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation.
    It is commendable that the authors have partially addressed this concern by including evaluation on the nuScenes dataset (Table 1, lines 177-184), demonstrating the generalization ability of V2B. This addition significantly strengthens the paper's experimental section. However, the initial concern regarding the robustness of results for less common categories (Van, Cyclist) on the limited KITTI test split still holds. While nuScenes provides a larger and more diverse dataset, a more comprehensive evaluation on its full test set (rather than just the validation set) or further exploration with other large-scale datasets like Waymo would provide even stronger evidence for the method's general applicability and robustness across varying object classes and environmental conditions.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper briefly touches upon the limitations in its conclusion, primarily by highlighting the method's effectiveness on sparse point clouds. A more explicit discussion of current limitations, such as potential failure modes in extremely dense or cluttered scenes, or computational overhead, could further enhance the paper. Negative societal impact is not discussed, which is acceptable for this type of technical contribution.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
