reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

Summary:
This paper introduces a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localize and follow individual objects of interest in sparse point clouds. The authors highlight the inherent challenge of 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR data, particularly in dynamic environments. The proposed V2B framework addresses this by first feeding a template (the object of interest) and a search area (the current frame's region of interest) into a shared Siamese network, utilizing PointNet++ as its backbone, to extract discriminative point features. Subsequently, it adaptively learns the correlation between the template and search area through a template feature embedding module, effectively encoding the template's characteristics into the potential target within the search area. A key component is the shape-aware feature learning network, which aims to characterize the object's 3D shape information by generating a dense point cloud from the typically sparse input, enhancing feature discrimination. For target localization, the approach innovatively decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center (x-y coordinates) and a z-axis center, regressing these in an anchor-free manner from a dense bird’s eye view (BEV) feature map, which is derived from the voxelized point cloud through max-pooling along the z-axis. The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset and demonstrate good generalization on nuScenes.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section, particularly the architectural diagrams in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3, can be followed easily, providing a good overview of the proposed V2B components. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? The abstract and introduction (Section 1) explicitly state the focus is on "3D single object tracking (SOT)," and the related work (Section 2.2) also delineates this scope. Despite this stated focus, I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination, data association, or robust occlusion handling mechanisms that are typical for even single-object trackers operating over long sequences, let alone multi-object systems. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. The method primarily focuses on localizing an object in the current frame given a template, which closely resembles a detection task. While Section 4.1 "Training and testing" describes a basic temporal update scheme ("For testing, we fuse the points inside the first GTBB and the previous result’s point cloud (if exists) as the template. Besides, we first enlarge the previous result by 2 meters in current frame, and then collect the points lying in it to generate the search area."), this is a very rudimentary form of tracking, essentially a sequential detection with a dynamically updated reference. This simple loop lacks the sophistication of typical tracking pipelines, which often involve Kalman filters for motion prediction, robust data association algorithms, and strategies for re-identifying objects after prolonged occlusions or out-of-view scenarios. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained, beyond this basic template update. Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, e.g., KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards. KITTI is predominantly known for its multi-object tracking benchmarks, and the evaluation metrics used in this paper do not align with those standard benchmarks, making direct comparisons difficult.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. While Section 2 covers "2D object tracking," "3D single object tracking," "3D multi-object tracking," and "Deep learning on point clouds," the actual experimental comparison in Section 4.2 focuses almost exclusively on three 3D SOT methods: SC3D [21], P2B [52], and BAT [83]. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChallenge or KITTI) are missed here. Although the paper explicitly states its focus on 3D tracking, the omission of more advanced 2D tracking techniques, especially those leveraging Siamese networks, limits the broader context. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the-work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. The paper includes a "3D multi-object tracking" subsection in its related work (Section 2.3) and mentions common tracking-by-detection schemes, yet no comparison is made against these methods. Given that the proposed framework's core localization component functions similarly to a 3D detector, comparing its performance within a full MOT pipeline or at least against strong 3D detectors would provide a more comprehensive validation of its capabilities in complex multi-object scenes. To name just few: X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene). A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB). A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021 (KITTI, NuScene). These works represent a significant body of recent research in 3D tracking, and their omission from the experimental comparison weakens the claim of achieving state-of-the-art results, especially in the context of autonomous driving where multi-object scenarios are the norm.

3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. The proposed V2B framework synthesizes several established techniques. The Siamese network architecture for feature extraction is a standard approach, with PointNet++ [51] used as a readily available backbone. The template feature embedding (Section 3.1.1) leverages common operations like MLP, max pooling, and cosine distance for similarity learning, which are well-known in feature correlation. The shape-aware feature learning network (Section 3.1.2) utilizes a gate mechanism (Eq. 5) for feature enhancement, feature expansion [79], EdgeConv [70] for local geometric information, and Chamfer distance [15] for training, all of which are existing components in point cloud processing and shape completion literature (e.g., SC3D [21]). Similarly, the voxel-to-BEV target localization network (Section 3.2) combines voxelization, 3D/2D convolutions, max-pooling along the z-axis, and anchor-free regression heads with Focal Loss [41] and L1 loss, drawing inspiration from existing 3D detection methods like AFDet [19]. While the specific combination and adaptation of these components for sparse point cloud SOT are presented, the individual modules do not introduce novel algorithmic or theoretical insights. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue, as the empirical validation, as discussed below, does not fully compensate for the lack of theoretical novelty.

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Specifically, the paper uses "Success" (IOU) and "Precision" (center distance error AUC) from the one-pass evaluation (OPE) [32], which are standard for single object tracking. However, KITTI is widely recognized for its multi-object tracking benchmark, which uses metrics like MOTA and HOTA. The choice to evaluate solely with SOT metrics, while valid for SOT, prevents a direct comparison with the broader KITTI leaderboard, which is a significant limitation for a paper claiming SOTA in the context of autonomous driving. Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? The authors state, "Since the ground truth of the test set of KITTI dataset cannot be obtained, we follow [21, 52] and use the training set to train and evaluate our method." (Section 4.1). This limits the generalizability and comparability of the results to official benchmarks. Furthermore, I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. The method evaluates performance on "car, pedestrian, van, and cyclist" (Table 1). While pedestrians and cyclists exhibit non-rigid motion, the template generation scheme ("fuse the points inside the first GTBB and the previous result’s point cloud") and the "dense ground truth processing" (Section 3.1.2) which concatenates and *aligns* points to form a fixed-size 2048-point "dense aligned 3D point cloud," seem implicitly designed for rigid or semi-rigid objects. Highly deformable objects or significant pose changes in non-rigid targets like humans would challenge this fixed-shape representation and dense point cloud generation, potentially leading to performance degradation. Testing on datasets specifically designed for human tracking, such as JRDB, would be crucial to validate its robustness to non-rigid deformations. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo? While the paper does report "generalization ability" on the nuScenes dataset (Section 4.2) by applying models trained on KITTI, the reported performance is significantly lower (e.g., Car Success 70.5% on KITTI vs. 31.3% on nuScenes in Table 1). This suggests that while there is some cross-dataset transfer, a full evaluation (including training and testing on nuScenes splits and comparison against nuScenes-specific SOTA) is missing. The complete absence of evaluation on the Waymo Open Dataset, another prominent benchmark for autonomous driving, further limits the comprehensive validation of the proposed method's real-world applicability and robustness across diverse environments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact in a dedicated section. A discussion on limitations would be beneficial to highlight scenarios where V2B might struggle, such as extremely sparse point clouds beyond the evaluated thresholds, very high-speed objects, or complex long-term occlusions that the current simple template update mechanism may not effectively handle. Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation and self-driving vehicles, where accurate perception is paramount for safety. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues. The ability to precisely localize and track individuals in 3D space from LiDAR data necessitates a discussion on potential misuses and privacy implications, emphasizing the importance of responsible AI development and deployment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper rigorously investigates the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms for supervised learning, focusing on two distinct scenarios for test-time tasks. First, the authors analyze the case where the test task is one of the `m` training tasks, each with `n` data points. For this scenario, the work proposes a novel definition of stability for meta-learning algorithms (Deﬁnition 3) that effectively captures the dependence on both the number of tasks (`m`) and the number of data points per task (`n`) in the generalization bound. This new approach, leveraging connections between algorithmic stability and generalization bounds, allows for a tighter characterization of the expected excess population loss. Second, the paper addresses the more challenging setting where the MAML model generalizes to an entirely unseen task. In this case, the authors formulate a generalization error bound (Theorem 3) that quantifies the performance based on the total variation distance (Deﬁnition 4) between the underlying distribution of the new task and those observed during the training process. The analysis is conducted under the assumption of strongly convex objective functions and a single adaptation step via SGD, with the primary objective of quantifying the test error of MAML's output, as outlined in the introduction.

**Main Review:**

**(Originality & Significance)**
I find that the paper presents a significant improvement over existing work and introduces novel theoretical results, which are highly valuable for the meta-learning community. In particular, the proposal of a new notion of stability (Deﬁnition 3) is a key contribution. This stability definition is meticulously crafted to measure the sensitivity of the algorithm to perturbations involving changing `K` data points in `S_in^i` and one data point in `S_out^i`, which directly relates to the data units involved in the MAML adaptation step. This allows the authors to construct a generalization error bound (Theorem 2) that explicitly depends on the number of data points per task (`n`), leading to a tighter `O(1/mn)` bound for strongly convex functions. As highlighted in Remark 1, this represents a substantial improvement compared to the `O(1/m)` bound achieved by previous work like [28], which did not account for `n`. The ability to capture the role of `n` provides a more granular and accurate understanding of MAML's generalization capabilities, especially in few-shot learning where `n` might be small but still crucial.

Beyond this, the paper also proposes a genuinely new approach of using the total variation distance (Deﬁnition 4) to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks. This directly addresses one of the core questions posed in the introduction: "Assuming that the task at test time is NOT one of the m tasks at training, how would the model perform on that task after the adaptation step?" The resulting bound in Theorem 3, which is independent of `K`, offers critical insights into how MAML's performance on unseen tasks is correlated with the similarity (or dissimilarity) of their underlying data distributions to the training tasks. This connection is also empirically aligned with observations from prior work such as [29]. This theoretical framework provides a valuable tool for understanding and potentially mitigating generalization issues in real-world scenarios where test tasks are often novel.

**(Quality)**
The paper is generally well-structured and well-written, presenting complex theoretical arguments in a clear and understandable manner. The decomposition of the test error into training and generalization errors (Section 2) provides a solid foundation for the subsequent analysis. The discussion around Lemma 1, establishing the strong convexity and smoothness of the MAML empirical loss, is particularly helpful for understanding the properties of the objective function being optimized.

The main weakness of the analysis lies in the assumption of the convex loss function (Assumption 1(i)), specifically that `ℓ(., z)` is µ-strongly convex. This implies that the meta-objective `ˆF` (and `F`) is also strongly convex, as established in Lemma 1. MAML is often applied to deep neural networks, whose loss landscapes are inherently non-convex. Therefore, the direct implications of such analysis to the real-world applications of MAML remain somewhat limited. However, I acknowledge that the paper explicitly justifies this weakness in Remark 2, stating that the strong convexity assumption is crucial for ensuring the meta-objective itself is convex, which is generally required for meaningful generalization results in this context. The authors also correctly identify that deriving generalization bounds for non-convex meta-functions is a natural, albeit challenging, future direction, as noted in Section 4. This transparency about the current limitations is appreciated.

Another limitation concerns the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The problem formulation in Section 2 (Equation 1) and the MAML algorithm (Algorithm 1) explicitly focus on a single step of SGD adaptation. While this simplifies the analysis significantly, MAML variants often employ multiple inner-loop gradient steps to achieve better adaptation. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1. Extending the stability definition (Deﬁnition 3) and its associated bounds to multiple inner-loop steps would likely introduce considerably more analytical complexity, as the recursive nature of these updates would need to be carefully propagated through the stability analysis. For instance, the second-order information in the gradient `∇ˆF_i(w, S_i)` (Equation 5) and its stochastic approximation `g_i(w; D_in^i, D_out^i)` (Equation 6) would become significantly more intricate with multi-step adaptations, potentially involving higher-order derivatives or more complex Jacobian terms. While existing works like [15] have studied multi-step MAML, they often rely on different assumptions (e.g., access to fresh samples), highlighting the specific challenges this paper's stability framework would face. A brief discussion on these analytical complexities and how they might affect the derived bounds would further strengthen the paper.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos that could be addressed for improved clarity and precision:
*   In Assumption 1(ii), the original review notes a potential typo regarding the term “unbounded”, though the manuscript text clearly states the gradient norm is “uniformly bounded”.
*   Line 196: The original review notes that “from [1]” should be from [24], though the manuscript correctly cites “[24] and [13]”.
*   Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
*   The original review notes a potential inconsistency in notation where “k” might have been used instead of “K” on lines 286 and 287, though the manuscript consistently uses “K” for batch size in these sections.
*   In the title of Section 3.3, “Task” is capitalized, which is a minor stylistic point.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms, a popular framework for few-shot learning. The authors investigate generalization from two crucial perspectives: first, when the task encountered at test time is one of the tasks observed during the training phase (referred to as recurring tasks), and second, when the test task is entirely new and previously unseen. For the recurring task scenario, the paper establishes a generalization bound under strong convexity assumptions, demonstrating that the generalization error decreases at a rate of $O(1/mn)$, where `m` is the number of training tasks and `n` is the number of samples per task. This bound offers a more refined understanding by incorporating `n`, which is often overlooked in prior stability analyses of meta-learning. In the case of unseen tasks, the authors derive a bound based on the total variation distance, illustrating how the generalization error is influenced by the proximity of the new task's underlying data distribution to those of the training tasks. The paper is exclusively theoretical, focusing on mathematical rigor, and does not include experimental validations.

**Main Review:**
Overall, I find this to be a strong theoretical contribution. The paper's strength lies in its comprehensive analysis of MAML's generalization capabilities across two distinct and practically relevant settings. The derivation of a generalization bound that explicitly accounts for the number of data points per task (`n`) in the recurring task case is particularly noteworthy. As highlighted in Remark 1 (line 290), this is a significant improvement over previous works, such as [28], which reported a $O(1/m)$ bound, by providing a tighter $O(1/mn)$ bound for strongly convex functions. This refinement offers a more granular understanding of how data availability impacts meta-learning performance, especially in few-shot learning where `n` is typically small. Furthermore, the total variation distance-based analysis for generalization to unseen tasks, as presented in Theorem 3, provides valuable insights into the conditions under which MAML can effectively transfer knowledge to novel scenarios, emphasizing the role of distribution similarity. While I did not conduct a line-by-line verification of all proofs, the core methodologies and resulting bounds appear sound and, to my current knowledge, represent novel advancements in the theoretical understanding of MAML.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1.  I have a significant query regarding the authors' statement in Remark 2 (line 292), which posits that the algorithmic stability technique is "mainly limited to the convex setting" for their analysis of recurring tasks. This assertion appears to contradict the broader literature on algorithmic stability, which has successfully extended to non-convex objectives in various contexts, as evidenced by works such as Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016), (London, 2017), and (Zhang et al., 2020). My understanding is that the unique definition of stability introduced in this paper (Definition 3, a (γ, K)-uniformly stable algorithm) is designed to capture the influence of the number of data points per task (`n`). However, it remains unclear why this specific definition or the overall MAML framework, even when the base loss `l(w, z)` is non-convex, would intrinsically prevent a meaningful stability analysis in non-convex settings without resorting to impractically small learning rates. The authors hint at this in Remark 2, stating that `l` being strongly convex and `α ≤ Ω(µ)` are needed "to ensure that the meta-objective is convex". Could the authors provide a more detailed explanation, perhaps with an illustrative example, of the precise challenges encountered when attempting to apply their novel stability definition (Definition 3) to non-convex meta-objectives? Specifically, how does the structure of `ˆF(w, S)` (equation 4) or `F(w)` (equation 2), which involves an inner adaptation step and an outer expectation/sum, complicate the stability analysis in the non-convex regime, making it distinct from the non-convex stability results in the cited literature for standard supervised learning?

2.  A more minor but still important point relates to the formal problem formulation. MAML's inherent structure, involving an inner-loop adaptation followed by an outer-loop meta-parameter update, naturally lends itself to a bilevel optimization framework. Many theoretical analyses of MAML-type algorithms, including [21] which is cited in the related work, explicitly adopt this bilevel structure (e.g., Ji et al., 2020). While this paper formulates the empirical problem as minimizing `ˆF(w, S)` (equation 4) which is an average loss after one step of SGD adaptation, it's worth exploring how the derived generalization bounds, particularly those based on the proposed (γ, K)-uniform stability (Definition 3), would adapt or extend to a more explicit bilevel programming formulation. Given that stability analysis in bilevel optimization is an active area of research (as indicated by the provided reference, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), I am curious if the authors foresee any specific theoretical challenges or necessary modifications to their stability definition and proof techniques to directly address a bilevel MAML setting. Would the current framework require significant re-conceptualization, or could it be adapted relatively straightforwardly?

**Updates:**
I appreciate the authors' detailed response to my question regarding the challenges of extending their stability analysis to non-convex settings. Their explanation, which clarified the distinction between general non-convex stability results and the specific complexities arising from their meta-objective formulation and novel stability definition, was satisfactory. I strongly recommend incorporating this expanded discussion, perhaps in an updated version of Remark 2 or a dedicated paragraph in Appendix G, to enhance clarity for future readers and proactively address similar questions.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper currently does not include a section discussing its limitations or potential societal impact. While this is primarily a theoretical work, a brief discussion of limitations, such as the strong convexity assumption (Assumption 1) and its implications for practical deep learning applications where objectives are often highly non-convex, would be beneficial. Similarly, a short statement on the (likely neutral) societal impact would complete the manuscript.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms for supervised learning problems. The core of their work focuses on characterizing MAML's generalization error from two distinct perspectives. First, they analyze the scenario where a new task encountered at test time is one of the `m` tasks observed during training, each with `n` data points. For this setting, and under strongly convex objective functions, they derive an expected excess population loss bound of $O(1/mn)$. Second, they address the more challenging case of MAML's generalization to an entirely unseen task. Here, they demonstrate that the resulting generalization error is influenced by the total variation distance between the underlying distributions of this novel task and the tasks used in the training process. The authors' proof techniques leverage connections between algorithmic stability and generalization bounds, notably by proposing a novel definition of stability tailored for meta-learning algorithms (Definition 3) which captures the distinct roles of both the number of tasks (`m`) and the number of samples per task (`n`). A single toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to empirically verify the impact of the sizes `m` and `n` on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.  This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. The problem formulation in Section 2, for instance, clearly distinguishes between population and empirical losses, and outlines the decomposition of test error into training and generalization components. The assumptions (Assumption 1 on loss functions, Assumption 2 on task distributions, and Assumption 3 on boundedness) are clearly stated and justified. Algorithm 1 provides a concise overview of the MAML process, aiding readability. The Related Work section also effectively positions this paper within the broader theoretical landscape of meta-learning.
2.  Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. MAML is a "popular meta-learning framework that has shown promise in practice" (Section 1), making theoretical understandings of its generalization crucial. The authors correctly identify a gap in previous analyses, noting that "at training stage, we often do not have access to fresh samples at every iteration," necessitating a "novel analysis that addresses this issue by characterizing the training error and generalization error of MAML separately." This work makes a solid attempt to fill this gap.
3.  This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training (`n`) is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. This is a significant contribution. As highlighted in Remark 1, the authors' "proposed uniform-stability deﬁnition empowers us to obtain a better bound and indicates the role of number of samples per task n in the generalization error." Specifically, their derived bound of $O(1/mn)$ (Theorem 2) for recurring tasks explicitly shows the dependence on `n`, which is an improvement over bounds like $O(1/m)$ achieved in prior work such as [28] for strongly convex functions. This demonstrates how their refined stability definition (Definition 3) effectively captures the granularity of data usage within the MAML framework.

Cons:
1.  My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training samples) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. Indeed, the authors themselves acknowledge this limitation in their discussion following Theorem 2, stating: "our bound is tight in the small K regime... However, one shortcoming of this result is that it is not tight in the large K regime." This suggests that while the $mn$ scaling is strong, the full parameter dependence may not be universally optimal. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. While the paper distinguishes itself from [28] by capturing the `n` dependency, similar to findings on how the support/query split used in meta-learning (including MAML) affects generalization (as highlighted in Chen et al., 2020), I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper that fundamentally alter our understanding beyond typical statistical learning rates.
2.  My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, and the theoretical analysis explicitly discusses its role (e.g., in the tightness of the bound for small vs. large K regimes, as per Theorem 2), I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task (Theorem 3), which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error, perhaps by systematically varying the distance between training and test task distributions. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection. Fourth, since supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it, as the current experimental setup only presents a toy example rather than a more practical meta-learning scenario.
3.  The training error characterization mainly follows from existing works, e.g., Fallah et al., 2020 [13], and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2002 [17]. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? For the training error (Proposition 1), the authors state it "is obtained by slightly modifying the well-known results on the convergence of SGD in [30–32] in order to take into account the stepsize constraints that are imposed by generalization analysis." A more detailed explanation of these specific step-size constraints and how they complicate the application of standard SGD convergence results to MAML's nested optimization structure would be beneficial. Regarding stability, the manuscript clearly articulates the challenge: "Deﬁnition 1 is useful for the case where we measure the performance of a model w by its loss function over a sample... However, in this paper we measure the performance of a model by looking at its loss after one step of SGD which involves K data points, as deﬁned in (6). Therefore, we cannot directly use Deﬁnition 1 for characterizing the generalization error of MAML." This necessitates their new stability definition (Definition 3), which accounts for changes in `K` data points for the inner adaptation and `1` data point for the outer loss, a key technical adaptation for the MAML structure.

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling (as discussed in Appendix F and referencing Hollander, 2012 [35]) and the introduction of a new stability definition (Definition 3) that better reflects the MAML architecture. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments and the theoretical rigor is commendable, I do not find the results universally interesting or exciting in terms of offering fundamentally new insights into meta-learning beyond what might be expected from standard statistical learning theory, and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people from this meta-learning area, I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML through empirical validation. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.
reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?

The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:

This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. - The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
The paper presents a comprehensive theoretical analysis of the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms, a prominent framework in meta-learning. A key contribution involves proposing a novel definition of algorithmic stability tailored for meta-learning, which allows the authors to characterize the generalization error of MAML in two crucial scenarios: first, when the test-time task is a "recurring" task drawn from the same distribution as the training tasks, and second, when it is a "new and unseen" task. This work significantly contributes to the theoretical understanding of MAML's performance boundaries, particularly by elucidating the roles of the number of tasks ($m$) and samples per task ($n$).

**Main Review:**
The authors have undertaken an ambitious theoretical study of MAML's generalization capabilities, offering valuable insights into its behavior under different task regimes. However, several critical points require further consideration and clarification to fully appreciate the practical implications of these theoretical findings.

**Please address the following concerns:**

-   **The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K.**
    *   The theoretical bounds derived, specifically in Theorem 2 (stability constant $\gamma = O(1) \frac{G^2(1+\alpha LK)}{mn\mu}$) and subsequently in Corollary 1 (test error $O(\frac{G^2(1+\alpha LK)}{mn\mu})$), indicate a direct proportionality between the generalization error and $K$, the number of labeled samples used for adaptation at test time. This suggests that as $K$ increases, the generalization error worsens, which is counter-intuitive and directly contradicts the common empirical observation in meta-learning literature where more adaptation data (larger $K$) typically leads to improved performance on a new task. This raises a significant concern about the tightness and practical relevance of the derived bounds, as they seem to diverge from practical outcomes. For instance, in few-shot learning, a switch from 1-shot to 5-shot learning almost invariably yields better accuracy. The manuscript should either reconcile this discrepancy, perhaps by explaining the specific regime in which this bound holds or by clarifying the interpretation of "generalization error" in this context, or demonstrate empirically that this theoretical behavior can indeed be observed under certain controlled conditions, even if not typical for standard meta-learning benchmarks. Without such clarification or empirical validation, the bound risks being vacuous for practical MAML applications.

-   **The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.**
    *   While the paper provides theoretical guarantees for the generalization error on recurring tasks, specifically the $O(1/mn)$ rate in Theorem 2 and Corollary 1, there is a distinct lack of empirical validation for these findings. To strengthen the paper's claims and demonstrate the practical implications of the derived bounds, it would be highly beneficial to include simple experiments. These experiments could involve varying the number of tasks ($m$) and the number of samples per task ($n$) in a controlled setting (e.g., using synthetic data or simple convex models where Assumption 1 holds) and then observing how the generalization error on a recurring task changes in accordance with the $O(1/mn)$ dependency. Such an empirical study would provide concrete evidence for the theoretical insights, especially regarding the roles of $m$ and $n$ in reducing generalization error, as highlighted in Remark 1 concerning the improved bound compared to the $O(1/m)$ achieved in [28]. This would help bridge the gap between theoretical findings and their practical manifestation.

-   **The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.**
    *   This concern specifically refers to the definition of $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability in Definition 3 on page 7, which states that $\tilde{S}$ differs from $S$ only for task $i$, by changing at most $K$ points in $S_i^{in}$ and one point in $S_i^{out}$. The output of the algorithm, $w_S$, is a global parameter obtained by minimizing the average empirical loss $\hat{F}(w, S) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^m \hat{F}_k(w, S_k)$ (Equation 4). When we modify $S_i$ to form $\tilde{S}_i$, the algorithm's output changes from $w_S$ to $w_{\tilde{S}}$. The question is whether the impact of this localized change to $S_i$ on the global solution $w_S$ is sufficiently contained such that the stability definition, which focuses on the loss difference for task $i$, remains valid and does not implicitly assume that $w_S$ and $w_{\tilde{S}}$ are "the same" or minimally different with respect to tasks $j \neq i$. Specifically, the expectation $E_A[\dots]$ is taken over the randomness of the algorithm $A$, which operates on the entire dataset $S$. A perturbation in $S_i$ will influence $w_S$ (the output of $A(S)$) across all tasks, not just $i$. The formulation implies that the stability bound holds for *any* task $i$ where the perturbation occurs, but the global nature of $w_S$ and $w_{\tilde{S}}$ needs to be carefully considered in how it propagates the local perturbation. A more detailed explanation of how the stability definition accounts for the global impact of local data changes would be beneficial.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
The authors have provided responses to the initial concerns, which have clarified some aspects of the work.

-   **While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$.**
    *   **When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?**
        *   The continued concern about the $K$ dependency in the generalization bound is critical. The authors' explanation that the $O(K/mn)$ bound derived from Theorem 2 is "tight in the small K regime" (page 7, after Theorem 2) but potentially loose in the large $K$ regime does not fully alleviate the concern. If, for small $K$ (e.g., $K=1$ or $K=5$ shots, typical in few-shot meta-learning), the bound is effectively no better than what one might expect from standard SGD on a single task (i.e., not leveraging the meta-learning aspect significantly better than a simple average), it undermines the theoretical justification for MAML's effectiveness in these settings. The core promise of meta-learning is its ability to generalize rapidly from *very few* samples per new task, meaning that $K$ is inherently small. A bound that shows degradation with increasing $K$ in this small regime is problematic for the practical interpretation of MAML.
    *   **Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.**
        *   The reference to Appendix E, which discusses improvements for the "large K regime," further highlights this disconnect. The bound in Appendix E suggests that improved performance for larger $K$ only manifests when $K$ is extremely large, on the order of thousands or tens of thousands. As the reviewer notes, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, a $K$ value of $\sim10,000$ implies that a significant fraction of the entire dataset is used for adaptation on a single task, which is far from the "few-shot" or "meta-learning" paradigm. This makes the theoretical improvements for large $K$ practically irrelevant for the target applications of MAML. The paper needs to better bridge this gap between theoretical bounds and the practical, small-$K$ scenarios that MAML is designed for, perhaps by identifying different theoretical regimes or providing a more nuanced interpretation of the current bounds.

**If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".**

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.
*   The paper's reliance on Assumption 1, which posits strong convexity, L-smoothness, and ρ-Lipschitz continuity of the Hessian for the loss function, presents a significant limitation regarding its applicability to modern deep learning architectures. While Remark 2 on page 8 briefly acknowledges that "the algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting" and that relaxing these assumptions would lead to a nonconvex meta-objective, this does not fully mitigate the concern. MAML is predominantly applied to deep neural networks, which are highly non-convex. The theoretical guarantees, therefore, apply to a simplified setting that may not accurately reflect the behavior of MAML in its most common use cases. Although the authors correctly identify extending this analysis to the nonconvex setting as crucial future work in Section 4, the current theoretical framework's practical scope is constrained by these strong assumptions. Further discussion on potential pathways or preliminary insights into how these results might extend, or fail to extend, to the non-convex domain would be valuable. Regarding societal impact, while the paper is primarily theoretical, robust theoretical understanding of meta-learning algorithms, even under simplifying assumptions, is a foundational step towards building more reliable and trustworthy AI systems, particularly for applications requiring rapid adaptation to new data and for understanding their generalization capabilities.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are clearly stated, societal impact is not mentioned.
