reviews.summary: This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.

Main Review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs) as a novel approach to tackle the critical problem of catastrophic forgetting in continual graph representation learning, specifically when new categories of nodes and their associated edges continuously emerge in a streaming fashion. The core innovation lies in its ability to adaptively extract and represent different levels of abstract knowledge through a hierarchical system of prototypes. To achieve this, the paper first introduces Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) that project raw node features and neighboring node topological information into distinct, elemental embeddings. These atomic embeddings are then used to dynamically refine or compose higher-level atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings. This hierarchical and compositional design is particularly well-suited for maintaining performance on existing knowledge while efficiently integrating new information. Extensive empirical studies on a diverse set of eight public datasets demonstrate that HPNs effectively mitigate catastrophic forgetting, achieve state-of-the-art performance, and are memory efficient on various continual graph learning tasks.

**Main Review:**
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1.  **The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.** The problem of continual graph representation learning, particularly with emerging node categories, is clearly articulated in the introduction, referencing real-world scenarios like citation networks and co-purchasing networks (lines 20-25). The introduction effectively differentiates this problem from dynamic graph learning and existing continual learning methods designed for Euclidean data (lines 50-60). The hierarchical prototype structure, as described in Section 2.3, provides an elegant mechanism for knowledge abstraction and retention. By representing nodes with a combination of atomic, node, and class-level prototypes (Equation 11), the model can adapt to new tasks by activating and refining only relevant prototypes, thereby avoiding the need to retrain the entire network. This compositional nature inherently supports scalability, allowing the model to handle large and evolving graphs efficiently, as further supported by the memory consumption analysis in Section 2.5 and Section 3.7.

2.  **Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.** The paper is well-structured, moving logically from problem definition (Section 2.1) through the architectural components (AFEs in Section 2.2, HPNs in Section 2.3) to the learning objective and theoretical analysis (Sections 2.4, 2.5). Figure 1, illustrating the overall framework of HPNs, is particularly helpful in understanding the multi-level prototype generation and refinement process. The detailed breakdown of how atomic features are extracted and how prototypes are matched, refined, and created (Equations 1-10) contributes to the clarity, making the complex ideas accessible to the reader.

3.  **The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.** The authors conducted experiments on eight diverse public datasets, including large-scale ones like OGB-Arxiv and OGB-Products (Section 3.1), which enhances the credibility of the findings. The comparison against a comprehensive set of baselines, including both graph-specific continual learning methods (ERGNN, TWP) and general continual learning techniques (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS) implemented on various GNN backbones (GCN, GAT, GIN), provides a robust benchmark (Table 1, Section 3.3). The use of Accuracy Mean (AM) and Forgetting Mean (FM) (Section 3.2), alongside the newly introduced Average Retaining Score (ARS) (Section 3.5), offers a nuanced evaluation of continual learning performance. The results clearly demonstrate HPNs' superior performance in terms of both accuracy and mitigating catastrophic forgetting (Table 1, Figure 3 left). Furthermore, the ablation studies (Tables 2 and 3, Section 3.4) effectively justify the contribution of each prototype level and loss term, while the parameter sensitivity analysis (Section 3.6, Figure 2) and memory consumption comparison (Table 4, Figure 3 right) empirically verify the theoretical claims regarding memory bounds and learning capability (Section 2.5). The visualization in Figure 4 also provides intuitive evidence for the interpretability of the learned representations.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1.  **The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.** The paper defines `x(v) ∈ Rdv` as the feature vector for node `v` (Section 2.1, line 119), encoding attributes like gender or nationality. The Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs), specifically `AFEnode = {Ai ∈ Rdv×da}` and `AFEstruct = {Rj ∈ Rdv×dr}` (Section 2.2, lines 125-126), are designed to operate on these relatively low-dimensional attribute vectors. However, in scenarios where node IDs are used as input features, `dv` would effectively become the total number of nodes `N`, and `Ai` and `Rj` would then represent embedding matrices of size `N x da` and `N x dr` respectively. With multiple AFEs (`la` and `lr`), this would lead to `(la + lr) * N * d_a/d_r` parameters, which could be substantially larger than the parameter count of a single embedding table used by other methods, thus undermining the claimed memory efficiency and scalability on very large graphs. It would be beneficial for the authors to explicitly state this assumption or discuss the implications if node IDs were to be used as input features.

2.  **Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?** The process described in Section 2.3 involves multiple steps that compute cosine similarities. Specifically, `SimMAXid` (Equation 5, lines 152-155) computes similarities to select relevant AFEs, then `SimE→A(v)` (Equation 6, lines 160-161) computes similarities for all selected atomic embeddings against `PA` (atomic prototype set). Following this, `Eold(v)` is determined based on a threshold `tA` (Equation 7), and then `Ldis` (Equation 8) is computed. Given that `PA` can grow as new prototypes are created (Equation 10), and `Eselect_A(v)` can contain `la' + lr'` atomic embeddings, these repeated pairwise similarity calculations between potentially many atomic embeddings and a growing set of prototypes could pose a significant computational bottleneck, especially during the training phase. Clarification on whether intermediate similarity results are cached or reused across these steps, or if there are other optimizations, would be valuable.

3.  **The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training.** The paper describes several instances of "hard selection" based on thresholds, such as the selection of top-`l'` AFEs based on `SimMAXid` (Section 2.3, lines 156-159) and the determination of `Eold(v)` (Equation 7, lines 163-166) and `Enew(v)` (Equation 9, lines 177-179) using the threshold `tA`. These operations, involving `max` functions and direct thresholding, are typically non-differentiable, or their gradients are zero almost everywhere. Algorithm 1 (lines 1-6) outlines a standard optimization loop, implying gradient-based training. It is critical to clarify how gradients are handled through these hard selection steps. Are straight-through estimators used, or is some form of relaxation applied (e.g., Gumbel-softmax)? Without this explanation, the end-to-end differentiability and thus the trainability of the entire system as described are questionable.

4.  **The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.** Equation 13 (`L = Ldis + Ldiv + Lcls`) combines the classification loss, divergence loss for AFEs, and distance loss for prototypes without explicit weighting coefficients. In multi-objective learning, it is standard practice to introduce hyperparameters (e.g., `λ1*Ldis + λ2*Ldiv + λ3*Lcls`) to control the relative importance of each loss term, as different losses often operate on different scales. The absence of such coefficients could lead to one loss dominating the optimization process, potentially hindering the effectiveness of the others. While Table 3 shows an ablation study on the *presence* of these loss terms, it does not address the lack of explicit weighting. A justification for this design choice, perhaps arguing that the scales of the losses naturally balance, or an empirical demonstration that weighting coefficients do not significantly improve performance, would strengthen this aspect of the paper.

5.  **As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:**
    *   Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”;
    *   Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
    *   Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
    *   Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an
    These minor grammatical and stylistic errors, while not impacting the technical soundness, slightly detract from the overall professional presentation of the manuscript. A thorough proofread by a native English speaker would be beneficial to address these issues before publication.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations with respect to some technical claims, such as the differentiability of the hard selection processes and the rationale behind the unweighted combination of loss functions. The authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications and details to enhance the clarity and robustness of the presented methodology. While the authors state that their work, focused on continual graph representation learning, has no potential negative societal impacts, it is worth acknowledging the broader ethical landscape of AI on graph data. Applications of graph learning, such as in social networks or recommender systems, can raise concerns regarding privacy, fairness, and the potential for reinforcing biases or enabling surveillance. Even if the current work does not directly engage with these issues, a brief reflection on these potential broader implications would align with current best practices in academic publishing.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.

Main Review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper addresses graph representation learning in a particular online/continual/streaming setting in which subgraphs are sequentially recieved which may contain nodes of categories/classes that haven't been seen before, while performance on the previously seen categories/classes must be maintained.  The approach involves extracting "atomic" features based on node features and local network structure, then constructing and maintaining prototype embeddings at various levels of abstraction, in order to produce a final embedding to be used by a node classifier. In each iteration the method decides whether to introduce new prototypes based on matching to previous prototypes. The authors prove a bound on the number of prototypes (hence, memory requirements) and show that the method will avoid catastrophic forgetting. Experimental results versus various baselines are positive.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: While graph representation learning has been heavily studied, including substantial work on temporal/dynamic networks, and continual/online learning has also received significant attention, the particular setting addressed in the paper is an interesting special case of the problem.  The proposed method has not been considered before, but it is not clear that broader insights arise from, or underpin, its development.  

Quality: Although intuitively appealing, the proposed model design is somewhat heuristic in nature.  It is unclear what fundamental principles motivate the use of prototypes or the various algorithmic choices which were made.  At least, the theoretical results in Section 2.5 show that the proposed method avoids certain pitfalls, but they do not otherwise guarantee how close to optimal the predictions are (e.g., in terms of regret).  The experimental results are a strength of the work, including good results in a systematic cross-validation-with-hyperparameter-tuning experiment compared to a number of baselines on multiple datasets, an ablation study, memory consumption, and t-SNE visualizations.

Clarity: The paper can be understood, with some effort.  There are relatively few typos or grammatical issues.  Figure 1, explaining the method, is helpful in understanding the approach.

Significance: The strong experimental results suggest that the proposed method is an improvement over the current state of the art at this particular representation learning problem, which may have practical importance.  However, the heuristic nature of the approach does not suggest that the ideas here will have influence beyond the narrow task, or will stand the test of time.

------

Thanks to the authors for their detailed and well argued rebuttal. The response was helpful for understanding how the authors perceive the points I've raised.   I have no problem with the hierarchical prototype approach proposed here, but I don't really see an overarching objective or motivating principle that spans both the matching prototypes phase and the learning phase, each of which seems to have unrelated objectives.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper addresses graph representation learning in a particular online/continual/streaming setting in which subgraphs are sequentially received which may contain nodes of categories/classes that haven't been seen before, while performance on the previously seen categories/classes must be maintained. The authors highlight that this is a critical problem given the continuous emergence of new categories of nodes and their associated edges in real-world applications like citation networks and co-purchasing networks, as mentioned in the introduction (lines 16-21). The proposed approach, Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), involves adaptively extracting different levels of abstract knowledge in the form of prototypes to represent continuously expanded graphs (lines 24-27). This is achieved by first leveraging a set of Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) to generate basic features encoding both attribute and topological information. HPNs then represent each node with three-levels of prototypes – atomic-level, node-level, and class-level – which are adaptively selected, composed, and stored (lines 28-29, 147-150). In each iteration, the method decides whether to introduce new prototypes based on matching to previous prototypes, ensuring that only relevant AFEs and prototypes are activated and refined (lines 29-31). The authors provide theoretical analysis on memory consumption (Theorem 1, lines 212-215) and continual learning capability (Theorem 2, lines 225-228), showing that the method avoids catastrophic forgetting and bounds memory usage. Extensive empirical studies on eight different public datasets justify that HPNs are memory efficient and achieve state-of-the-art performance on various continual graph representation learning tasks.

Main Review:
Originality: While graph representation learning has been heavily studied, including substantial work on temporal/dynamic networks, and continual/online learning has also received significant attention, the particular setting addressed in the paper is an interesting special case of the problem. The authors clearly differentiate their problem from dynamic graph works, which primarily concern time-dependent graphs, and from existing continual learning techniques, which often fail to appropriately consider both feature information and topological structure in a graph context (lines 42-46, 58-60). The proposed method has not been considered before, but it is not clear that broader insights arise from, or underpin, its development. The overall design, while novel, feels somewhat tailored to the specific problem definition rather than being derived from a more general theoretical framework for continual learning.

Quality: Although intuitively appealing, the proposed model design is somewhat heuristic in nature. It is unclear what fundamental principles motivate the use of prototypes or the various algorithmic choices which were made. For instance, the definition of Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) as learnable linear transformations (lines 119-122) and the specific form of the divergence loss (Ldiv, Eq. 4, lines 132-135) for decorrelating AFEs appear to be pragmatic choices rather than being derived from a deeper theoretical basis. Similarly, the adaptive selection and refinement of prototypes based on cosine similarity thresholds (tA, tN, tC) (Eq. 7, line 163; also lines 189-190) are crucial to the method but lack a clear principled motivation for their precise formulation or the choice of threshold values, beyond empirical tuning. The overall loss function, a combination of Ldis, Ldiv, and Lcls (Eq. 13, line 203), also appears to be an empirically effective aggregation of individual objectives rather than stemming from a unified objective. At least, the theoretical results in Section 2.5 show that the proposed method avoids certain pitfalls, but they do not otherwise guarantee how close to optimal the predictions are (e.g., in terms of regret). Specifically, Theorem 1 provides an upper bound on the number of prototypes and thus memory consumption (lines 212-215), and Theorem 2 suggests conditions to prevent catastrophic forgetting (lines 225-228). While valuable for understanding the model's behavior, these theorems primarily establish existence and bounds, not performance guarantees or optimality, an aspect acknowledged by the authors who state the bound in Theorem 2 is "not tight" (line 233).

The experimental results are a strength of the work, including good results in a systematic cross-validation-with-hyperparameter-tuning experiment compared to a number of baselines on multiple datasets, an ablation study, memory consumption, and t-SNE visualizations. HPNs consistently outperform various baselines, including specialized continual graph learning methods like ERGNN and TWP, as well as general continual learning techniques (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS) adapted for GNNs (Table 1, lines 276-281). Notably, HPNs achieve positive forgetting mean (FM) on several datasets (e.g., +0.6% on Cora, +2.9% on OGB-Products), indicating positive knowledge transfer, and significantly outperform joint training on large datasets like OGB-Products (Table 1, line 297). The ablation study in Table 2 clearly demonstrates the incremental benefit of each hierarchical prototype level (A-, N-, C-prototypes) on both accuracy mean (AM) and forgetting mean (FM) (lines 301-303). Table 3 further supports the contribution of the individual loss terms (Lcls, Ldiv, Ldis) to overall performance (lines 304-309). The analysis of parameter sensitivity in Figure 2(a) and (b) empirically verifies the theoretical insights from Theorem 2 regarding the impact of AFE numbers and prototype dimensions (lines 325-331). Memory consumption is also a strong point, with HPNs accommodating all tasks on the largest dataset, OGB-Products, with a notably small amount of parameters compared to baselines (Table 4, lines 339-340), and actual usage remaining well below the theoretical upper bound (Figure 3, right, lines 341-344). Finally, the t-SNE visualizations in Figure 4 provide compelling qualitative evidence that HPNs learn well-separated and interpretable node representations across tasks (lines 349-352).

Clarity: The paper can be understood, with some effort. The multi-level prototype architecture, the adaptive selection of AFEs, and the interplay of different loss functions (Lcls, Ldis, Ldiv) require careful reading. However, the overall presentation is clear, with relatively few typos or grammatical issues. Figure 1, explaining the overall framework of HPNs, is particularly helpful in visually breaking down the process from incoming subgraphs to AFE selection, prototype matching, and the final hierarchical representation used for classification (lines 98-102).

Significance: The strong experimental results suggest that the proposed method is an improvement over the current state of the art at this particular representation learning problem, which may have practical importance. The ability of HPNs to effectively handle continuously emerging new categories of nodes in large-scale graphs, such as OGB-Products with millions of nodes and multiple tasks, while maintaining memory efficiency, is a significant practical contribution (Table 4, lines 339-340). However, the heuristic nature of the approach, particularly the lack of a unifying theoretical principle for its various components, does not suggest that the ideas here will have influence beyond the narrow task, or will stand the test of time as a foundational framework for continual learning.

------

Thanks to the authors for their detailed and well-argued rebuttal. The response was helpful for understanding how the authors perceive the points I've raised. I have no problem with the hierarchical prototype approach proposed here, but I don't really see an overarching objective or motivating principle that spans both the matching prototypes phase and the learning phase, each of which seems to have unrelated objectives. While the ablation study (Table 3) shows that combining Ldis, Ldiv, and Lcls improves performance, and the authors explain that Ldiv pushes AFEs apart and Ldis makes embeddings closer to prototypes (lines 307-309), this describes the *effect* of these objectives rather than a single, coherent theoretical principle that unifies the prototype matching/creation logic with the multi-objective learning process. The decision to match prototypes based on cosine similarity thresholds (Eq. 7) and then refine them with Ldis (Eq. 8) alongside decorrelating AFEs with Ldiv (Eq. 4) and classifying with Lcls (Eq. 12) feels like a pragmatic construction rather than a derivation from a singular, deeper theoretical foundation.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Graph representation learning can be subject to algorithmic bias/fairness issues. Although the authors mention limitations in the conclusion and appendix regarding theoretical aspects (checklist 1b), the discussion on potential negative societal impacts (checklist 1c) was minimal. It would be worth mentioning how this might be addressed in future work. Continual learning on graph data, especially in domains with sensitive information (e.g., social networks, recommendation systems), could inadvertently amplify existing biases present in the data or introduce new ones as new categories and their associated topological structures are continuously incorporated. The prototype-based approach, if not carefully designed or monitored, could lead to biased representations for underrepresented groups or perpetuate harmful stereotypes if the learned prototypes reflect and reinforce societal biases embedded in the training data.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the continuous representation learning problem for graph data. The main goal is to learn emerging novel node categories, and simultaneously maintain the learned knowledge over existing graphs. In the proposed solution called Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), several levels of general knowledge are extracted, in the form of atomic, node and class-level prototypes. A theoretical analysis for the memory consumption and continual learning capability is presented. Empirical study is conducted  on eight different public datasets, including large-scale datasets with millions-scale nodes.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points

- The paper addresses an important, well motivated problem in graph representation learning with practical significance. 

- The presentation is largely clearly. 

- Experiments are fairly comprehensive, including results on large-scale graphs.

Weak points

- There is some similarity to few-shot learning on graphs [a,b], although they mostly focus on novel class only without considering how the performance of existing classes are maintained. Nevertheless, discussion and comparison with few-shot methods should be included, to highlight the advantage of HPN over few-shot methods.

[a] Meta-GNN: On Few-shot Node Classification in Graph Meta-learning. CIKM19

[b] Relative and Absolute Location Embedding for Few-Shot Node Classification on Graph. AAAI21.

- Hierarchical graph representation learning has also been explored [c,d], which is more flexible/self-adaptive than the 3-level prototypes in this paper.  

[c] Hierarchical graph representation learning with differentiable pooling. In NIPS 2018

[d] Graph Few-shot Learning via Knowledge Transfer. AAAI20.

- The model involves a number of hyperparameters related to the thresholds/number of candidates, including l_a, l_r, l'_a, l'_r, t_A, t_N, t_C. This would make model tuning slow and impractical. It is also possible that, as novel classes emerge, these hyperparameters may not to change as well for optimal performance.

- Message passing GNN is considered SOTA on graph data. The proposed solution derives representations from multi-hop neighbors, lacking the recursively defined message passing mechanism. A discussion on this is needed to demonstrate the design advantages and disadvantages of HPN.

-- update after rebuttal --

Based on the rebuttal, i have increased my rating slightly.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Fine with me.

[a] Meta-GNN: On Few-shot Node Classification in Graph Meta-learning. CIKM19

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces **Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs)**, a novel approach designed to tackle the challenging problem of continual graph representation learning. This problem is particularly relevant in dynamic real-world scenarios where new categories of nodes, along with their associated edges, continuously emerge in existing graphs, such as new research areas in citation networks or new product types in co-purchasing networks. The primary objective of HPNs is to effectively learn representations for these emerging novel node categories while simultaneously ensuring that the knowledge acquired about existing nodes and classes is robustly maintained, preventing catastrophic forgetting. The proposed solution achieves this by adaptively extracting and organizing different levels of abstract knowledge in the form of atomic, node, and class-level prototypes, as detailed in the abstract (lines 28-30) and Section 2.3 (lines 142-146). The authors provide a theoretical analysis of the model's memory consumption and its continual learning capabilities, which is a valuable contribution. Furthermore, the empirical study is conducted on eight diverse public datasets, including large-scale graphs like OGB-Products (with over 2 million nodes and 47 categories, lines 92-93, 246-247), demonstrating the practical applicability and scalability of HPNs.

**Main Review:**

**Strong points**

*   **The paper addresses an important, well-motivated problem in graph representation learning with practical significance.** The introduction effectively highlights the limitations of traditional GNNs on static graphs and the critical need for solutions that can handle continuously emerging node categories. Examples from citation networks (new research areas) and co-purchasing networks (new products) (lines 19-22) clearly illustrate the real-world relevance of preventing performance interruption on existing nodes while integrating new information (lines 23-25, 60-61). This challenge of effectively incorporating new feature and topological information without forgetting prior knowledge is a core problem in continual learning on graphs.

*   **The presentation is largely clear.** The manuscript is generally well-structured and easy to follow. The breakdown of the HPN framework into its two core modules—Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) and Hierarchical Prototype Networks—in Section 2, supported by Figure 1, provides a clear conceptual understanding. The problem statement and notation in Section 2.1 are also precisely defined, aiding reader comprehension.

*   **Experiments are fairly comprehensive, including results on large-scale graphs.** The experimental setup covers a broad range of 8 datasets, encompassing various types such as citation networks (Cora, Citeseer, OGB-Arxiv), web page networks (Wisconsin, Cornell, Texas), an actor co-occurrence network, and a product co-purchasing network (OGB-Products) (Section 3.1, lines 243-247). The inclusion of large-scale datasets, notably OGB-Arxiv (169,343 nodes, 1,166,243 edges) and OGB-Products (2,449,029 nodes, 61,859,140 edges) (lines 246-247), is particularly commendable, demonstrating the scalability and efficiency of HPNs. The comparison against a diverse set of baselines, including both graph-specific continual learning methods (ERGNN, TWP) and general continual learning techniques (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS), implemented with multiple GNN backbones (GCN, GAT, GIN), provides a robust evaluation (lines 278-280).

**Weak points**

*   **There is some similarity to few-shot learning on graphs [a,b], although they mostly focus on novel class only without considering how the performance of existing classes are maintained. Nevertheless, discussion and comparison with few-shot methods should be included, to highlight the advantage of HPN over few-shot methods.** While few-shot learning on graphs addresses the challenge of learning new classes from limited data, its primary focus is often on the performance of these *novel* classes, or on adapting a model to a new task efficiently. HPNs, on the other hand, are explicitly designed for *continual learning*, where the critical challenge, as stated in the abstract (lines 23-25) and introduction (lines 60-61), is to *maintain performance over existing nodes and tasks* while incorporating new ones. This distinction, particularly regarding catastrophic forgetting and retention of previously learned knowledge, is crucial. A dedicated discussion differentiating HPNs from few-shot methods, explaining why few-shot techniques might fall short in a true continual learning setting (e.g., lack of mechanisms to prevent forgetting of *old* classes), would significantly strengthen the paper and clarify its unique contributions within the broader landscape of learning with limited and evolving data.

*   **Hierarchical graph representation learning has also been explored [c,d], which is more flexible/self-adaptive than the 3-level prototypes in this paper.** The HPN framework relies on a fixed three-level prototype structure: atomic-level, node-level, and class-level (lines 28-30, 142-146). While this structure offers interpretability and a clear mechanism for knowledge retention, it may lack the flexibility of more adaptive hierarchical methods that can dynamically determine the optimal number or granularity of abstraction levels based on the evolving graph structure or task complexity. The paper could benefit from a discussion on how this fixed-level hierarchy compares to more flexible hierarchical approaches, particularly in scenarios where the natural hierarchical organization of knowledge within the graph might not neatly align with these three predefined levels. This could involve exploring the limitations or advantages of a fixed hierarchy in capturing diverse or complex multi-scale patterns in different graph types.

*   **The model involves a number of hyperparameters related to the thresholds/number of candidates, including l_a, l_r, l'_a, l'_r, t_A, t_N, t_C. This would make model tuning slow and impractical. It is also possible that, as novel classes emerge, these hyperparameters may not to change as well for optimal performance.** The HPN architecture introduces several critical hyperparameters that govern the selection and creation of prototypes. These include `la` and `lr` for the cardinality of atomic feature extractors (lines 122-124), `l'a` and `l'r` for the number of selected AFEs (lines 155-157), and the matching thresholds `tA`, `tN`, and `tC` for atomic, node, and class-level prototypes respectively (lines 163, 179-181). While the paper mentions that `tA`, `tN`, and `tC` are selected via cross-validation from a set of values (lines 262-264), the sheer number of these interacting parameters presents a significant challenge for practical deployment and tuning. Optimizing this many hyperparameters can be computationally expensive and time-consuming, especially for large-scale graphs. Furthermore, the assumption that these optimal values remain static as new categories and tasks continually emerge is a strong one. It is plausible that the ideal configuration of these thresholds and AFE counts might need to adapt to the characteristics of new incoming data, which is not directly addressed.

*   **Message passing GNN is considered SOTA on graph data. The proposed solution derives representations from multi-hop neighbors, lacking the recursively defined message passing mechanism. A discussion on this is needed to demonstrate the design advantages and disadvantages of HPN.** The HPN framework, particularly through its Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs), obtains atomic node embeddings by applying linear transformations to node features and atomic structure embeddings from sampled multi-hop neighbors (Eq. 1 and Eq. 2, lines 125-128). This approach directly processes features from neighbors. In contrast, state-of-the-art GNNs like GCN, GAT, and GIN (which are used as baselines in the paper, lines 278-280) leverage a recursive message passing mechanism that iteratively aggregates and transforms information from a node's local neighborhood, allowing for more complex and context-dependent feature interactions across multiple layers. While HPN's design may offer advantages in modularity and explicit prototype learning for continual adaptation, it is important to discuss the potential trade-offs. The current design might not fully capture the intricate, non-linear dependencies and feature enrichments that arise from iterative message passing. A dedicated section or paragraph discussing why HPN opts for this specific representation derivation over a standard GNN message passing paradigm, and outlining the implications (both advantages in terms of continual learning and potential disadvantages in expressive power or local structural awareness), would provide valuable context.

**-- update after rebuttal --**

Based on the rebuttal, I have increased my rating slightly. The authors clarified some aspects regarding the practical implications of the hyperparameters and the distinction from few-shot learning, which was helpful. However, the fundamental concerns regarding the extensive hyperparameter tuning and the comparison of the representation learning mechanism with standard GNN message passing still persist to a degree that prevents a higher rating.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
Fine with me. The authors have stated in the checklist (1c, lines 494-496) that their work addresses a continual graph representation learning problem and does not foresee any potential negative societal impacts, which seems reasonable given the technical nature of the contribution.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper addresses the continuous representation learning problem for graph data. The main goal is to learn emerging novel node categories, and simultaneously maintain the learned knowledge over existing graphs. In the proposed solution called Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), several levels of general knowledge are extracted, in the form of atomic, node and class-level prototypes. A theoretical analysis for the memory consumption and continual learning capability is presented. Empirical study is conducted  on eight different public datasets, including large-scale datasets with millions-scale nodes.

Main Review:
Strong points

- The paper addresses an important, well motivated problem in graph representation learning with practical significance. 

- The presentation is largely clearly. 

- Experiments are fairly comprehensive, including results on large-scale graphs.

Weak points

- There is some similarity to few-shot learning on graphs [a,b], although they mostly focus on novel class only without considering how the performance of existing classes are maintained. Nevertheless, discussion and comparison with few-shot methods should be included, to highlight the advantage of HPN over few-shot methods.

[a] Meta-GNN: On Few-shot Node Classification in Graph Meta-learning. CIKM19

[b] Relative and Absolute Location Embedding for Few-Shot Node Classification on Graph. AAAI21.

- Hierarchical graph representation learning has also been explored [c,d], which is more flexible/self-adaptive than the 3-level prototypes in this paper.  

[c] Hierarchical graph representation learning with differentiable pooling. In NIPS 2018

[d] Graph Few-shot Learning via Knowledge Transfer. AAAI20.

- The model involves a number of hyperparameters related to the thresholds/number of candidates, including l_a, l_r, l'_a, l'_r, t_A, t_N, t_C. This would make model tuning slow and impractical. It is also possible that, as novel classes emerge, these hyperparameters may not to change as well for optimal performance.

- Message passing GNN is considered SOTA on graph data. The proposed solution derives representations from multi-hop neighbors, lacking the recursively defined message passing mechanism. A discussion on this is needed to demonstrate the design advantages and disadvantages of HPN.

-- update after rebuttal --

Based on the rebuttal, i have increased my rating slightly.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Fine with me.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: RotoGrad: Gradient Homogenization in Multi-Task Learning

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a method to reduce the optimization difficulty for multi-task learning. The key idea is to homogenize task gradients in terms of both magnitudes and directions. Specifically, they rescale task gradients to maintain similar convergence rates over tasks. At the same time, they introduce new network parameter R_k for each task k and train it so that the angle between task-specific and average gradients are minimized when task-head parameters are rotated using R_k. The method improves baselines and outperforms existing methods on DIGITS, CIFAR10, NYUv2, and CelebA datasets.
reviews.main_review:
I think the key contribution of this paper is to introduce rotation matrices that reduces gradient conflicts by rotating the feature-space z in a way that matches their directions. In particular, the idea to use rotation matrices is reasonable and good in that they will preserve the relational information (e.g. distance) between features.

They claim that the novelty lies on the joint homogenization of gradients in terms of both magnitude and direction. However, IMTL [18] also jointly addressed loss magnitudes and gradient direction. Rather, I think novelty lies on the way they formulate the problem as a Stackelberg game. In other words, they optimize rotation matrices only to match gradient directions while optimizing the remaining network parameters to minimize the task loss. GradNorm used a similar strategy but they focused on adjusting gradient magnitudes. 

In the experiments, their method improves baselines and performs better than other methods on four datasets and for two different backbone architectures. Additional analysis on training stability, effect of sub-dimension size for rotation, and training time comparison provides more comprehensive views.
However, it seems one needs to carefully adjust the training parameters for the rotation matrix, depending on dataset. when I ran the submitted code for mnist, the median cosine similarity for the task 'density' oscillated largely.


I think L130-131 is a bit misleading, because rotating the feature z is different from rotating its gradient g. Rotating z could result in the change of magnitudes in gradients. For example, consider a function f(x,y) = [relu(x),0]. A vector [x,y]=[1,0] and its rotation [x,y] = [-1,0] have different gradient magnitude.

It would be good to add ablation study to analyze the effect of magnitude and direction separately, i.e., magnitude-only and direction-only. Since each of them was explored in existing work, comparing each to corresponding other approaches will be also informative, e.g., magnitude-only to GradNorm and direction-only to PCGrad and GradDrop.

One way to demonstrate the training behavior will be to visualize the actual reduced gradient conflicts, possibly using angles, compared to the baseline. Also, comparing the method with baselines in the task loss curve will show that the method results in lower task loss.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces RotoGrad, a novel algorithm designed to mitigate optimization difficulties commonly encountered in multi-task learning (MTL) due to negative transfer. The core innovation lies in its approach to jointly homogenize task gradients, addressing disparities in both their magnitudes and directions. Specifically, RotoGrad employs a mechanism to dynamically rescale task gradients, ensuring tasks converge at more similar rates by prioritizing slower-converging tasks, as detailed in Section 3.1 and Equation 2. Concurrently, it introduces a task-specific rotation matrix, $R_k$, for each task $k$, which operates on the shared feature space $z$ to produce $r_k = R_k z$ (Figure 2, L129-131). These rotation matrices are explicitly trained to minimize the angle between the task-specific gradients and a target average gradient, thereby aligning gradient directions (Equation 3). The authors demonstrate that RotoGrad consistently improves upon baselines and outperforms several existing MTL methods across a diverse set of problems, including multi-label classification on CelebA and CIFAR10, and complex computer vision tasks (semantic segmentation, depth estimation, surface normals) on the NYUv2 dataset, as well as multi-digit classification on MNIST.

**Main Review:**
I believe the key contribution of this paper is to introduce rotation matrices ($R_k$) that reduce gradient conflicts by smoothly rotating the feature-space $z$ in a way that matches their directions. This mechanism, where $R_k$ transforms the shared representation $z$ into a task-specific representation $r_k = R_k z$ before passing it to the task head (Figure 2), is central to RotoGrad's ability to align gradients in the long run (L37-38). In particular, the idea to use rotation matrices is reasonable and good in that they are chosen from the special orthogonal group $SO(d)$ (L131), which ensures proper rotation and will inherently preserve the relational information (e.g., distance) between features in the shared representation space. This design choice is a theoretically sound way to modify gradient directions without distorting the underlying feature geometry.

The authors claim that the novelty lies on the joint homogenization of gradients in terms of both magnitude and direction. However, IMTL [18] also jointly addressed loss magnitudes and gradient direction, albeit through a different mechanism involving scaling gradients based on their projections and initial magnitudes. Rather, I think a significant aspect of RotoGrad's novelty lies on the way they formulate the problem as a Stackelberg game (L149). This interpretation allows them to derive clear guidelines for training convergence, specifically by setting the rotation optimizer as a "slow-learner" compared to the network optimizer (L150-152, Appendix B). This theoretical grounding for stability, supported by empirical evidence in Section 6.1, distinguishes their approach. In contrast, GradNorm [6] used a similar strategy of learning dynamic weights but they focused primarily on adjusting gradient magnitudes to balance learning rates across tasks, without directly addressing directional conflicts through feature space rotation.

In the experiments, their method consistently improves baselines and performs better than other methods on four datasets (MNIST, CIFAR10, NYUv2, CelebA) and for two different backbone architectures (e.g., ResNet18 and a convolutional network for CelebA in Table 4, SegNet for NYUv2 in Table 3). For instance, on the NYUv2 dataset, RotoGrad achieves the best performance in segmentation and depth tasks and significantly improves surface normals, notably being the only method to yield an average positive task improvement over training single-task models independently (Table 3, L320-321). Similarly, for CelebA, RotoGrad convincingly outperforms all competing methods across all f1-score statistics for both convolutional and ResNet18 backbones (Table 4, L339-340). Additional analysis on training stability (Section 6.1), the effect of sub-dimension size for rotation (Section 6.2), and training time comparison (Table 4) provides more comprehensive views of the method's practical implications. The empirical validation of the Stackelberg game's convergence condition in Section 6.1 (Figure 3) is particularly insightful, showing that setting the rotation learning rate to half of the network's learning rate (5e-4) leads to optimal performance for the MNIST tasks (L271-272). Furthermore, the analysis on rotating a subspace of the shared feature `z` (Section 6.2, Table 1) effectively demonstrates RotoGrad's scalability, showing that even rotating only 64 features (12.5% of the 512-dimensional `z` for CIFAR10) still yields better results than vanilla optimization, which is crucial for applications with very high-dimensional shared representations (L281-282). The computational overhead is also reasonable, with RotoGrad staying on par with other strong baselines in training time (Table 4, L346-348), which is impressive given the additional optimization for rotation matrices.

However, it seems one needs to carefully adjust the training parameters for the rotation matrix, depending on the dataset. While the paper identifies a "sweet-spot" for the rotation learning rate on MNIST (L271-272), my observation when running the submitted code for MNIST showed that the median cosine similarity for the 'density' task oscillated largely. This suggests that the optimal `ηroto` might be highly sensitive to the specific task set or dataset characteristics, and finding this optimal balance might not be as straightforward in practice as implied, potentially requiring extensive hyperparameter tuning. The sensitivity shown in Figure 3 reinforces this point, where even small deviations from the optimal learning rate lead to higher and noisier test errors.

I think L130-131, which states that RotoGrad "smoothly rotates the shared feature space differently for each task, seamlessly aligning gradients in the long run," is a bit misleading, because rotating the feature $z$ is different from rotating its gradient $g$. While the rotation matrices $R_k$ directly act on the feature $z$ to produce $r_k$, and the objective $L_{rot}$ (Equation 3) aims to align the gradients $\nabla_{r_k} L_k$ with a target vector $v_n$, the overall effect on $\nabla_z L_k$ via the chain rule ($g_{n,k} = R_k^T \tilde{g}_{n,k}$) is more complex than a simple "rotation" of the gradient direction while preserving magnitude. Rotating $z$ could result in the change of magnitudes in gradients. For example, consider a function $f(x,y) = [\text{relu}(x),0]$. A vector $[x,y]=[1,0]$ and its rotation $[x,y] = [-1,0]$ have different gradient magnitudes (e.g., $\nabla_x f$ would be 1 for $x=1$ and 0 for $x=-1$, assuming ReLU). This nuance should be clarified to avoid misinterpretation of how $R_k$ influences the gradients of $L_k$ with respect to $z$.

It would be good to add an ablation study to analyze the effect of magnitude and direction separately, i.e., magnitude-only and direction-only. The current "IMTL-G+Rk" baseline (L314, Table 1) is a step in this direction, but it's not a pure direction-only method since $R_k$ is optimized for task loss rather than gradient alignment. A clean ablation, where one component is enabled while the other is disabled, would provide clearer insights into the individual contributions of RotoGrad's two building blocks (Section 3.1 and 3.2). Since each of these aspects was explored in existing work, comparing each to corresponding other approaches will be also informative, e.g., comparing a magnitude-only variant of RotoGrad to GradNorm [6] and IMTL-G [18], and a direction-only variant to PCGrad [33] and GradDrop [7]. This would further solidify the argument for the benefits of their joint approach.

One way to demonstrate the training behavior will be to visualize the actual reduced gradient conflicts, possibly using angles (e.g., cosine similarity between task gradients and the target vector $V$ from L111), compared to the baseline methods. While Figure 1 provides an excellent qualitative illustration on synthetic problems, visualizing this on real datasets (e.g., CIFAR10 or NYUv2) over training epochs would offer compelling quantitative evidence of RotoGrad's effectiveness in aligning gradients. Also, comparing the method with baselines in the task loss curve for individual tasks will show that the method results in lower task loss and more stable convergence, complementing the aggregated performance metrics (like $\Delta_k$) presented in the tables.

**Rating:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes RotoGrad, a multi-task optimisation strategy that normalises the gradient magnitude and (conflicting) direction in a unified manner, to avoid the negative transfer. The proposed method is intuitive, theoretically motivated by the two player-game, and hyper-parameter free (except for learning rate required for updating rotation parameters). 

The paper extensively evaluates the proposed method comparing with other baselines, both in single domain MTL setting NYUv2, and in multi-domain MTL setting CIFAR-10, and CelebA, and have showed improved performances in all settings.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is clearly written, and well-motivated from the existing Stackelberg leadership model. I also appreciate the authors discuss the memory and time complexity in Section 3.4, and being honest about the current limitation on scaling issues in computer vision tasks, which typically with large representation dimensions, requiring extensive computing time for an exact solution without any approximation.

Generally, I like the idea and particularly for the part that introducing the gradient rotation, which I believe is novel and overlooked in previous multi-task optimization papers I know. Here are some other comments, which I hope the authors could clarify and hopefully could further improve the paper.

-- Subspace rotation. As mentioned in the previous comment, for computing a direct solution requires a $d^2$ rotation matrix for each task with representation dimension $d$. The introduced rotation parameters can be neglectable if $d$ is small. However, if $d$ is large as typically in most computer vision tasks, it introduces more parameters compared to baseline methods, which leads to unfair comparison.  We thus would not know whether the improved performance is based on the design of this method, or simply because of the increased number of learnable parameters. In Table 1, applying the rotation only in the 64 subspace is having a close and worse performance compared to the baseline performance, this verifies my concern here. To be clear on the parameter size and/or FLOPs in each method would be essential for a fair comparison. Maybe adding a linear embedding to map to a smaller embedding size, or applying a block-wise rotation would alleviate this issue?

-- Limitation on multi-task network design. From my understanding, the proposed RotoGrad requires a clear top-down multi-task network design with a **single** shared representation output $z$, which the task-specific rotation function needs to be applied on. It would be tricky, or impossible? to apply this strategy to any design of the network, say MTAN as discussed in the related work, or other popular multi-task network design: such as AdaShare, Cross-Stitch network, from which the shared network parameters are defined implicitly. In this way, the RotoGrad would be very hard to optimise, maybe requiring network-specific modification on choosing the appropriate $z$. This is not a serious issue though, if the improved performance solely on hard parameter sharing is significant enough that surpass the improvements brought by the design of the network itself. A paragraph discussing this issue would be helpful.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: This paper studies the theoretical insights and optimisation strategy for multi-task learning, and thus having no societal impact.

7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces RotoGrad, a novel multi-task optimization strategy designed to address the critical issue of negative transfer in multi-task learning (MTL). RotoGrad achieves this by proposing a unified mechanism that normalizes both the gradient magnitude and (conflicting) direction across tasks. The proposed method is intuitive, theoretically motivated by framing the optimization as a two-player game between the network and the rotation parameters, and is notably hyper-parameter free for gradient magnitude homogenization (except for the learning rate required for updating the rotation parameters).

The paper extensively evaluates the proposed method, comparing its performance against several baselines across diverse MTL settings. These include single-domain MTL problems like those on the NYUv2 dataset for computer vision tasks, and multi-domain scenarios such as multi-label classification in CIFAR-10 and CelebA. Across all these settings, RotoGrad consistently demonstrates improved performance, showcasing its effectiveness in mitigating negative transfer.

**Main Review:**
The paper is clearly written, presenting its ideas and methodology in an accessible manner. The motivation for RotoGrad is particularly well-articulated, stemming from the existing challenges of negative transfer due to gradient disparities, as highlighted in the introduction (lines 16-22). The theoretical foundation, drawing parallels to the Stackelberg leadership model (Section 3.2, Equation 4), provides a robust framework for understanding the joint optimization of network and rotation parameters, which is a significant strength. I also appreciate the authors' thorough discussion of the memory and time complexity in Section 3.4, which is crucial for practical implementation. Their honesty about the current limitations related to scaling issues in computer vision tasks, which typically involve large representation dimensions and thus extensive computing time for an exact solution without approximation, is commendable and adds to the paper's credibility.

Generally, I like the core idea of RotoGrad and particularly the innovative approach of introducing gradient rotation. This aspect, I believe, is novel and has been largely overlooked in previous multi-task optimization papers. While existing methods often focus on magnitude normalization or simple gradient projection, RotoGrad's mechanism to "smoothly rotate the shared feature space differently for each task, seamlessly aligning gradients in the long run" (lines 30-32) offers a more sophisticated way to address directional conflicts. This distinct approach contributes significantly to the field. Here are some other comments, which I hope the authors could clarify and hopefully could further improve the paper.

--- **Subspace rotation.** As mentioned in the previous comment, for computing a direct solution, a full `d x d` rotation matrix is required for each task, given a representation dimension `d`. This means `K * d(d-1)/2` additional parameters for `K` tasks when using the Lie algebra parametrization (Section 3.4, line 174). The introduced rotation parameters can be negligible if `d` is small. However, if `d` is large, as is typically the case in most computer vision tasks (e.g., `d=512` for ResNet18 on CIFAR10, or `d=2048` for ResNet18 on CelebA), these additional parameters can become substantial compared to baseline methods, potentially leading to an unfair comparison. We thus would not know whether the improved performance is based on the fundamental design of this method, or simply because of the increased number of learnable parameters. In Table 1, applying the rotation only in the 64-dimensional subspace (`m=64` out of `d=512` features, representing only 12.5% of the shared feature space) is having a close and slightly worse performance (`avgk ∆k = 2.90 ± 0.49`) compared to the baseline vanilla performance (`avgk ∆k = 2.73 ± 1.37`), while the full rotation (`RotoGrad 512`) yields significantly better results (`avgk ∆k = 4.48 ± 0.99`). This observation from Table 1 indeed verifies my concern here, indicating a clear trade-off between computational overhead/parameter count and performance gains. To be clear on the parameter size and/or FLOPs in each method would be essential for a truly fair comparison, especially given the acknowledged `O(Kd^3)` time complexity for rotation updates (lines 177-178). For instance, providing a column in Tables 1, 3, and 4 detailing the total learnable parameters or FLOPs for each method would greatly enhance the transparency of the comparison. Maybe adding a linear embedding to map the shared feature `z` to a smaller embedding size before applying the rotation, or applying a block-wise rotation (as briefly alluded to in line 186) would alleviate this issue by reducing the effective `d` for rotation without sacrificing too much performance.

--- **Limitation on multi-task network design.** From my understanding, the proposed RotoGrad requires a clear top-down multi-task network design with a **single** shared representation output `z` (as depicted in Figure 2 and described in Section 2, lines 60-61), which the task-specific rotation function `R_k` needs to be applied on. This hard-parameter sharing architecture is a specific design choice. It would be tricky, or perhaps even impossible, to directly apply this strategy to any arbitrary design of the network, say MTAN as discussed in the related work, or other popular multi-task network designs such as AdaShare or Cross-Stitch networks, from which the shared network parameters are defined implicitly or distributed across multiple layers. For instance, MTAN uses task-specific attention modules at various points in the network, and Cross-Stitch networks learn linear combinations of activations from parallel task branches. In such architectures, the concept of a single, distinct `z` at the "end of the backbone" (lines 60-61) might not exist or be easily identifiable. In this way, RotoGrad would be very hard to optimize without significant architectural modifications, perhaps requiring network-specific adaptations on choosing the appropriate `z` or defining the point of rotation. This is not a serious issue though, if the improved performance solely on hard parameter sharing is significant enough that it surpasses the improvements brought by the design of the network itself, which seems to be the case in the presented experiments with SegNet and ResNet18 backbones. Nevertheless, a dedicated paragraph discussing this architectural dependency and the scope of applicability (e.g., in the conclusion or a dedicated discussion section) would be helpful to set clear expectations for future users and researchers.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
This paper studies the theoretical insights and optimization strategy for multi-task learning, and thus having no direct negative societal impact.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes RotoGrad, a multi-task optimisation strategy that normalises the gradient magnitude and (conflicting) direction in a unified manner, to avoid the negative transfer. The proposed method is intuitive, theoretically motivated by the two player-game, and hyper-parameter free (except for learning rate required for updating rotation parameters). 

The paper extensively evaluates the proposed method comparing with other baselines, both in single domain MTL setting NYUv2, and in multi-domain MTL setting CIFAR-10, and CelebA, and have showed improved performances in all settings.

Main Review:
The paper is clearly written, and well-motivated from the existing Stackelberg leadership model. I also appreciate the authors discuss the memory and time complexity in Section 3.4, and being honest about the current limitation on scaling issues in computer vision tasks, which typically with large representation dimensions, requiring extensive computing time for an exact solution without any approximation.

Generally, I like the idea and particularly for the part that introducing the gradient rotation, which I believe is novel and overlooked in previous multi-task optimization papers I know. Here are some other comments, which I hope the authors could clarify and hopefully could further improve the paper.

-- Subspace rotation. As mentioned in the previous comment, for computing a direct solution requires a $d^2$ rotation matrix for each task with representation dimension $d$. The introduced rotation parameters can be neglectable if $d$ is small. However, if $d$ is large as typically in most computer vision tasks, it introduces more parameters compared to baseline methods, which leads to unfair comparison.  We thus would not know whether the improved performance is based on the design of this method, or simply because of the increased number of learnable parameters. In Table 1, applying the rotation only in the 64 subspace is having a close and worse performance compared to the baseline performance, this verifies my concern here. To be clear on the parameter size and/or FLOPs in each method would be essential for a fair comparison. Maybe adding a linear embedding to map to a smaller embedding size, or applying a block-wise rotation would alleviate this issue?

-- Limitation on multi-task network design. From my understanding, the proposed RotoGrad requires a clear top-down multi-task network design with a **single** shared representation output $z$, which the task-specific rotation function needs to be applied on. It would be tricky, or impossible? to apply this strategy to any design of the network, say MTAN as discussed in the related work, or other popular multi-task network design: such as AdaShare, Cross-Stitch network, from which the shared network parameters are defined implicitly. In this way, the RotoGrad would be very hard to optimise, maybe requiring network-specific modification on choosing the appropriate $z$. This is not a serious issue though, if the improved performance solely on hard parameter sharing is significant enough that surpass the improvements brought by the design of the network itself. A paragraph discussing this issue would be helpful.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
This paper studies the theoretical insights and optimisation strategy for multi-task learning, and thus having no societal impact.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a novel method for the multitask learning environment, which deals with the conflicting gradients problem (AKA negative transfer). The method comprises two parts for dealing with gradient magnitudes and directions. The latter part is quite novel, well-motivated (with a cartoon example from Fig. 1), and theoretically sound. The method is tested against a number of MTL benchmarks and is shown to produce better-performing models. Overall, the paper presents interest to the MTL community.
reviews.main_review:
L27: why is gradient cancellation seen as a problem? If a model cannot explain training data, conflicting gradients can be seen as reducing the variance of the solution, yielding a model which is "equally" bad wrt multiple objectives (as shown in Fig.1). In such a case, the proposed solution can be seen as an efficient way to increase model capacity in the right places (where the heads branch out), thus leading to a model with higher capacity (which is not bad per se but requires acknowledgment).

L35: can prioritization of the "lagging" tasks be harmful, e.g., in cases where the task loss is noisy (e.g., due to extreme label noise)?

The usage of a task-specific rotation matrix is interesting; however, could it be replaced with a simpler learned construct, such as a task-conditional batch norm, keeping the alternating training scheme from Alg.1 intact?

The usage of matrix exponential for parameterizing the rotation matrix is interesting; however, in the light of the argument in L151, do the gradients of the unconstrained parameters of R require any treatment (gradient magnitude/LR adjustment)?

One unclear aspect associated with matrices R is whether the preceding activations are the output of a linear layer or a nonlinearity. If activations are nonlinear and given that such a matrix R constitutes an additional linear layer, it would be interesting to see extended single-task baselines with matrix R added in the right place (where it would otherwise branch out from the backbone in MTL).

An ablation study of the magnitude and direction counterparts, particularly where another more traditional method can be applied in place of one of the proposed magnitude components, is missing. The closest I could find in Table 1 reporting IMTL-G+$R_k$

================= Post-rebuttal comment

After reading all reviews and responses, and discussing the common concerns with the other reviewers, one weak point of this work remains the increased capacity of the network (regardless of how the added parameters are optimized). For this reason, I chose to keep my original rating of WA.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
